EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed over parts of Central Texas by the first of February. Some of these locations missed out on beneficial rains which occurred across much of the region, allowing severe drought conditions to linger through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions were ongoing at the beginning of February and continued through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several weeks with little or no rain and dry air, with rainfall totals at 10 to 25 percent of average (average for the period in the area is approximately 3 inches), deteriorated drought conditions to severe (D2) across the ranchlands of extreme southeastern Brooks and extreme southwestern Kenedy County by March 27th. A burst of locally heavy rainfall on the late evening of the 28th and early morning of the 29th would improve conditions at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weeks of little to no rainfall, leaving departures from monthly averages (January and February through mid March) at 10 to 25 percent of the 3 inch or so average for the period, resulted in a brief period of Severe (D2) Drought in southeastern Brooks County during the final few days of March. A band of heavier rainfall from late evening on the 28th through early morning of the 29th would improve conditions for the start of April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of few upper level disturbances to cross south Texas and the Rio Grande Valley in spring 2018 did so late on March 28th, creating welcome rainfall for parts of the region where drought had worsened.  Starr County, which had seem some late winter rainfall to keep drought at bay, was hit again - this time with local hailstorms across the eastern half of the county.  Large hail up to golfball size was reported around Rio Grande City and points east just after 7 PM.  Other hailstorms formed in southeast Jim Hogg County, as well as northern and northeastern Zapata County later that evening, but there were no reports available as of this writing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Facebook reports relayed to the NWS office in Brownsville reported two large hail reports east of Rio Grande City: Golfball sized hail 4.6 miles east, near La Puerta, and half-dollar sized hail (1.25) 4 miles east southeast of Rio Grande City near Los Velas road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Airport Wildfire began around 1300CST about two miles north of Borger Texas in Hutchinson County near the Hutchinson County Airport. The wildfire consumed approximately one thousand acres. There were no reports of any homes or other structures threatened, damaged or destroyed by the wildfire. There was a report of one injury however no fatalities were reported. There were a total of nine fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire including the Texas A&M Forest Service. The wildfire was contained around 0100CST on March 24.

The combination of abnormal dryness, dry fine and medium fuels, a near flash drought day that preceded it, and near critical humidity with gusty east winds aided the development of wildfire on the Santa Ana NWR along the Rio Grande in southern Hidalgo County.  The fire was ignited by torching - embers that floated across the Rio Grande from a nearby sugar burn in northern Tamaulipas, Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local emergency management, fire departments, and the US Fish and Wildlife Service reported a 300 acre wildfire developed rapidly during the afternoon of March 7th, when embers from a sugar cane burn in northern Tamaulipas, Mexico, just south of the Rio Grande drifted into the refuge and began the process of torching brush and grass. The wildfire, aided by dry fuels, gusty east winds, and near critical relative humidity levels, continued for nearly 48 hours before being 100 percent contained.  The refuge was closed to public operations through the following weekend for safety precautions and continued clean-up activities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ninth Avenue Wildfire began about six miles east of Bushland Texas in Potter County around 1758CST. The wildfire was caused by downed power lines and burned towards northwest Amarillo which resulted in the Amarillo Medical District being threatened with evacuations being ordered. There were no reports of any injuries or fatalities. There were fifty homes and twenty-six other structures that were threatened by the wildfire but were saved. There were no reports of any homes or other structures damaged or destroyed by the wildfire. The wildfire consumed an estimated four hundred acres. There were a total of fourteen fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire including the Texas A&M Forest Service. The resources assigned included a TFS taskforce and a TIFMAS engine strike team. The wildfire was contained around 0600CST on March 19.

The Old Muddy Road Wildfire began about nineteen and a half miles northwest of Amarillo Texas in Potter County around 1815CST. The wildfire was caused by downed power lines and consumed four thousand and four hundred and eighty acres. There was a report of twenty homes threatened and thirteen homes destroyed by the wildfire. There were also forty other structures threatened and ten other structures destroyed. There were no reports of any injuries or fatalities. There were a total of nine fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire including the Texas A&M Forest Service. The  resources assigned included two TFS taskforces, two TIFMAS engine strike teams, two heavy air tankers, lead plane and an aerial supervision platform. The wildfire was contained around 1100CST and 1200CST on March 19.

The Gravel Pit Wildfire began about seven miles north of Amarillo Texas in Potter county around 1518CST. The wildfire consisted of three fires: one near Dumas Texas, another fire in Potter County near the Fain Gas Plant, and another fire near Masterson Texas. The Fain Gas Plant, the Blue Knight Oil Plant, and the J. Lee Milligan Asphalt Plant were evacuated. The wildfire consumed two thousand nine hundred and seventy-six acres. The cause of the wildfire was determined to from faulty vehicle equipment or vehicle dragging a chain. There were ten homes and forty other structures that were threatened by the wildfire but were saved, however there were no homes or other structures destroyed by the wildfire. There also no reports of injuries or fatalities. The wildfire was contained around 0100CST to 0130CST March 16. There were a total of two fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire including the Texas A&M Forest Service. The resources assigned to the wildfire included a TFS taskforce, TIFMAS engine strike team, three single engine air tankers, an aerial supervision platform and other support overhead.

The County Line Wildfire began around 1708CST about four or five miles south southeast of Channing Texas in Oldham County. The wildfire was caused by smoking materials which was started in open field. The wildfire consumed fifteen thousand six hundred and eighty-two acres in Hartley, Oldham and Moore Counties. There were two homes and five other structures threatened by the wildfire but were saved. There were no reports of any homes or other structures damaged or destroyed by the wildfire. There were also no reports of any injuries or fatalities. There were a total of twelve fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire including the Texas A&M Forest Service. The resources assigned included two TFS task forces, three single engine air tankers, one heavy air tanker and an aerial supervision platform. The wildfire resulted in evacuations for residents near the Moore County and Hartley County line. The wildfire was contained between 0400CST and 0500CST March 16.

The Ponder���s Place Wildfire began around 1451CST about ten miles west of Dumas Texas in Moore County. The cause of the wildfire was determined to be from pasture and field burning. The wildfire consumed one thousand two hundred and sixty-one acres. There was a report of one home and one other structure threatened by the wildfire and one other structure was reported destroyed. There were no reports of any injuries or fatalities. There were a total of six fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire. The wildfire was contained around 1930CST.

The West Follett Wildfire began about one mile west of Follett Texas in Lipscomb County around 1316CST. The wildfire consumed approximately two thousand acres. The wildfire burned towards the western side of Follett and one hundred and eighty-two homes and ten other structures were threatened and evacuations were ordered. There were several plastic fertilizer tanks and a grain elevator that burned down. There were no reports of any injuries or fatalities. There were no reports of any homes damaged or destroyed. There were a total of twelve fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire including the Texas A&M Forest Service. The wildfire was contained around 1745CST and 1815CST.

The Channing Wildfire began about four miles north northeast of Channing Texas in Hartley County around 1717CST. The cause of the wildfire was downed power lines and power poles. The wildfire started after twelve power poles were downed by the high winds near Farm to Market Road 722 and US Highway 385 in an open field. There was a report of two homes, five barns, windmills and an oil infrastructure threatened although no homes or other structures were reported damaged or destroyed. The wildfire consumed an estimated forty-five hundred acres. There were a total of five fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire including the Texas A&M Forest Service. The resources assigned included a TFS taskforce, three single engine air tankers, a heavy air tanker, lead plane and an aerial supervision platform. The wildfire was contained around 0000CST and 0100CST on March 19.

The FM 2381 wildfire began around 1736CST about three miles north of Bushland Texas in Potter county. The wildfire started just east of Farm to Market Road 2381 north of the Bushland High School. The wildfire consumed a measured four hundred and ninety-six acres. There were roughly fifteen to thirty homes threatened however no homes or other structures were damaged or destroyed. There were also no reports of injuries or fatalities.  Farm to Market Road 2381 and Farm to Market Road 1061 had been closed due to low visibilities. There were a total of two fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire including the Texas A&M Forest Service. The wildfire was contained around 0900CST on March 3.

The County Line South Wildfire began around 1030CST about two miles south of Shamrock Texas in Wheeler County. The cause of the wildfire was a vehicle dragging a chain. There were two homes and two other structures threatened however no homes or other structures were damaged or destroyed by the wildfire. There was one near miss, however there no injuries or fatalities. The wildfire consumed three hundred and twenty-five acres. There were a total of five fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire. The wildfire was contained around 1330CST to 1400CST.

The 1062 Wildfire began about three miles north of Dawn Texas in Deaf Smith County around 1205CST. The wildfire consumed an estimated three hundred acres and began at the Top of Texas Cotton Gin north of Dawn and burned in grass fuels. There were no reports of any homes or other structures threatened and there were also no reports of any homes or other structures damaged or destroyed. There were no reports of any injuries or fatalities. There was a report that one hundred and twenty cotton modules burned. The wildfire was contained around 1500CST. The Texas A&M Forest Service, along with several other fire departments and fire agencies, responded to the wildfire. The resources assigned included a TFS dozer strike team.

A upper level low developed across the far NE Texas Panhandle with an associated dryline back to the south and southwest. A jet streak working into west Texas during the afternoon helped bring strong shear throughout the column into the Panhandles. In particular, noticeable surface based directional shear right along the dry line, west winds to the west and east winds east of the dryline helped storms develop rotation along the converging boundary. Storms that developed had broad rotation along a small line of individual couplets. Some deeper cores did produce some severe hail across the northeastern Panhandles. A landspout was confirmed by spotters in far southeast Ochiltree County along with an  6 mile weak EF-1 tornado track through parts of Hemphill and Lipscomb counties.

The Carson County Gin Wildfire began about two to three miles west southwest of White Deer Texas in Carson County near the Carson County Gin around US Highway 60 and County Road X around 1442CST. The wildfire was caused by burning sawdust piles. There were reports of one home and fifteen other structures threatened by the wildfire but were saved, however no homes or other structures were destroyed. There was one report of an injury, however no fatalities were reported. The wildfire consumed seven hundred and seventy-one acres. There were a total of seven fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire including the Texas A&M Forest Service. The resources included a dozer strike team. The wildfire was contained around 2300CST.

The Tut Creek Wildfire began around 1500CST about four miles northwest of Lefors Texas in Gray County. The wildfire is located approximately seven miles southeast of Pampa Texas. There were no reports of homes or other structures threatened, damaged or destroyed by the wildfire and there were also no reports of any injuries or fatalities. The cause of the wildfire was reported to be from faulty oil field equipment. The wildfire consumed seven thousand three hundred and thirty-four acres. There were a total of ten fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire including the Texas A&M Forest Service. The resources included a TFS Taskforce, a TIFMAS engine strike team, four single engine air tankers and an aerial supervision platform. The wildfire was contained around 2300CST.

The Arrington Ranch 2 Wildfire began around 1505CST about eight miles south southeast of White Deer Texas in Carson County. The wildfire was caused by downed power lines and consumed approximately three hundred and fifty acres. There were no reports of any homes or other structures threatened, damaged or destroyed by the wildfire. There were also no reports of any injuries or fatalities. There were a total of five fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire which was contained around 2000CST.

A warm front lifted north into Eastern Oklahoma and Central Arkansas during the morning hours of February 24th, resulting in a large warm and humid air mass across the Ark-La-Tex region. Meanwhile, showers and thunderstorms increased across North Texas during the morning as well, ahead of a strong upper trough that ejected east across the Southern Plains and Mississippi Valley. This trough also drove an attendant cold front into East Texas during the afternoon, with numerous showers and thunderstorms noted along and ahead of the front. The air mass ahead of the front was moderately unstable, allowing an isolated severe thunderstorm to develop over Western Harrison County Texas, which downed power lines between Hallsville and Marshall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down between Hallsville and Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong but shallow cold front moved through East Texas during the afternoon hours of February 10th, with temperatures falling to or below freezing during the late evening hours. Weak overrunning atop this shallow arctic air mass resulted in patchy freezing drizzle developing around midnight throughout the early morning hours on February 11th across portions of Northeast Texas, resulting in icing of bridges across Lake Cypress Springs in Franklin County, as well as vehicles and elevated objects in Red River and Smith Counties. ||Temperatures remained at or below freezing across much of Northeast Texas during the daytime hours on February 11th, as a weak upper level disturbance traversed Northern and Eastern Texas. Scattered areas of light freezing rain mixed with sleet fell across portions of Northeast Texas, with light accumulations of sleet on grassy and road surfaces, as well as bridges and overpasses throughout Smith, Wood, and Upshur Counties. Elsewhere across Northeast Texas, only trace amounts of freezing rain and sleet were recorded, with very little if any impacts observed. ||In Smith County, 0.2 inches of sleet was recorded at Tyler, as well as areas from Lake Palestine near the Teaselville community to Flint, Whitehouse, and Bullard. In Wood County, 0.1 inches of sleet was recorded in Mineola and Quitman. In Upshur County, 0.1 inches of sleet was recorded near Big Sandy.

A cold front moved southeast across Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and Northern Louisiana during early morning hours of February 6th. Meanwhile, overrunning increased during the daytime hours which led to the development of scattered showers behind the front, which developed into embedded thunderstorms as upper level disturbances shifted northeast across the area enhancing large scale lift, ahead of an upper level shortwave trough which moved into the Southern Plains during the afternoon from the Four Corners region. Steep lapse rates ahead of this shortwave trough contributed to elevated instability aloft, with deep layer shear also adequate enough for some organized post-frontal thunderstorms within a cool air mass with surface temperatures in the lower 50s. As a result, a few of these storms became severe, producing numerous reports of small and large hail across portions of Northeast Texas during the afternoon hours. The strong to severe thunderstorms diminished by early evening as the post-frontal rains became more widespread across the area, thus diminishing the elevated instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell on the southwest wide of Lindale.

A cold front moved southeast across Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and Northern Louisiana during early morning hours of February 6th. Meanwhile, overrunning increased during the daytime hours which led to the development of scattered showers behind the front, which developed into embedded thunderstorms as upper level disturbances shifted northeast across the area enhancing large scale lift, ahead of an upper level shortwave trough which moved into the Southern Plains during the afternoon from the Four Corners region. Steep lapse rates ahead of this shortwave trough contributed to elevated instability aloft, with deep layer shear also adequate enough for some organized post-frontal thunderstorms within a cool air mass with surface temperatures in the lower 50s. As a result, a few of these storms became severe, producing numerous reports of small and large hail across portions of Northeast Texas during the afternoon hours. The strong to severe thunderstorms diminished by early evening as the post-frontal rains became more widespread across the area, thus diminishing the elevated instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mount Vernon Herold Newspaper reported pea to quarter size hail covering the ground in Downtown Mount Vernon.

An upper level low pressure system progressed east southeast from the Pacific Northwest into the Central and Southern Rockies on the morning of February 20th, reinforcing a cold front slowly southeast into Southeast Oklahoma, Northeast Texas, and Southwest Arkansas during the early morning hours of February 21st. A deep southwest flow aloft was present along and behind the front across the Southern Plains and Mississippi Valley, with weak embedded disturbances in the flow helping to enhance the development of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms along and behind the front. A warm, moist, and weakly unstable air mass spread north ahead of the front into Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, and North Louisiana, with the southwest flow aloft tapping into the subtropical jet, thus transporting Pacific moisture northeast along and behind the front. Periods of moderate to at times, heavy rainfall fell along and behind the front across Northeast Texas during the evening hours on the 20th through much of the 21st before gradually diminishing during the late evening hours. However, additional periods of moderate to heavy rain spread across much of East Texas on February 22nd north of the cold front, and continued through the morning hours on the 23rd. Widespread rainfall amounts of four to in excess of nine inches fell across much of East Texas during this 3 day period, resulting in widespread flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water on FM 1254 northeast of Mineola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Winter Storm impacted the Big Country during the morning and early afternoon of February 21.  With surface temperatures in the middle to upper 20s, heavy sleet mixed with freezing rain fell across the Big Country.  This freezing precipitation resulted in numerous school closures and traffic accidents.  As the heavy precipitation ended by early afternoon on February 21, light freezing drizzle continued through the night across the Big Country into the early afternoon on February 22.  The weight of the ice resulted in some downed tree branches across the area.  ||The heavy sleet and freezing rain abated by the early afternoon on February 21 and became freezing drizzle as it continued across the remainder of West Central Texas, south of Interstate 20 during the night of February 21 and into the morning on February 22.  This continued to cause ice to accumulate on some roads, bridges, elevated objects and trees.  ||These conditions lead to a multiple vehicle pileup on Interstate 10 near Sonora.  Many school districts across West Central Texas began school about two hours late on February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and sleet fell at the airport and across Tom Green County on the morning of February 21.  Freezing drizzle returned during the overnight hours into the morning of February 22.  This freezing rain did collect on elevated objects like trees.  Some branches broke under the weight of the ice.  Many schools started classes by 10 AM on February 22.

This continued to cause ice to accumulate on some roads, bridges, elevated objects and trees.  ||These conditions lead to a multiple vehicle pileup on Interstate 10 near Sonora.  Many school districts across West Central Texas began school about two hours late on February 22.

This freezing rain did collect on elevated objects like trees.   Bronte and Robert Lee Independent School Districts started classes by 10 AM on February 22.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A multiple vehicle pileup occurred on Interstate 10 on the morning of February 22 around 6 AM between Mile Markers 400 and 401 near Sonora.  Vehicles involved were cars, pickups, commercial tractor trailers and pumpers.  They were scattered across the median as well as east and west bound lanes of the interstate at two separate sites.  This resulted in the closing of about 3 miles of Interstate 10 through noon.   Traffic was rerouted.  There were some injuries, but no fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall during the final week of February resulted in moderate flooding across the Sabine River of Northeast Texas near Mineola across Southern Wood and Northern Smith Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall of eight to ten inches across the Sabine River basin of Northeast Texas during the final week of February resulted in rapid rises on the Sabine River south of Mineola, with the river cresting at 18.69 feet during the evening hours on February 27th. This resulted in moderate backwater flooding along the river, inundating some barns and secondary roads including FM 1804 in Southern Wood and Northern Smith Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought a shallow layer of subfreezing air to South Central Texas. Isentropic lift of warm moist air over this shallow cold layer led to wintry precipitation.  Most of the precipitation was freezing rain and sleet, but there was some snow toward the end of the event. There were reports of 1/8 inch of ice accumulated in Leon Valley, New Braunfels, and San Geronimo. Icy roads were a problem across the region. Ice closed many roads across the region and caused numerous vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of January 30th four grass and brush fires started near Wimberley. The Ledgerock, 8080,  Balke, and Woodcreek Fires were managed as the 2325 Wildland Fire. Weather on the 30th was clear and dry with relative humidity between 25% and 30% and winds from the south-southeast at 8-11 mph. Some homes were evacuated as a precaution, but no structures were damaged. The fire eventually consumed 750 acres before being contained on January 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Highway 354 wildfire began around 1634CST about ten miles west of Four Way Texas in Moore county. The wildfire started between South County Line Road and Middlewell Road close to Farm to Market Road 354 and consumed approximately twenty-five hundred acres. The cause of the wildfire was unknown. There were no reports of homes or other structures damaged or destroyed and there were no reports of injuries or fatalities. There were a total of four fire departments and other fire agencies that responded to the wildfire including the Texas A&M Forest Service and the wildfire was contained around 2030CST to 2100CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure spread east across the Southern Plains during the evening hours of January 15th, with a arctic air mass quickly spilling south into the Ark-La-Tex during the late afternoon through the evening hours behind a strong cold front. Scattered areas of light rain developed along and just behind the front over much of East Texas during the evening hours, with the rain quickly changing over to a mixture of freezing rain and sleet, before eventually transitioning over to snow during the early morning hours on the 16th. While the mixed wintry precipitation melted during the beginning of the event, temperatures fell quickly into the mid 20s by or shortly after daybreak on the 16th, resulting in some black ice development on secondary roadways, bridges and overpasses, with snowfall accumulations ranging from a dusting up to a half inch. ||Here are the snowfall reports from this event across Deep East Texas: In Angelina County, 6 miles north of Hudson recorded 0.1 inches of snow. In Nacogdoches County, 5 miles east southeast of Appleby recorded 0.5 inches of snow. In Shelby County, Center recorded 0.5 inches of snow. In San Augustine County, San Augustine recorded 0.3 inches of snow. In Sabine County, Hemphill recorded 0.2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought a shallow layer of subfreezing air to South Central Texas. Isentropic lift of warm moist air over this shallow cold layer led to wintry precipitation.  Most of the precipitation was freezing rain and sleet, but there was some snow toward the end of the event. There were reports of 1/8 inch of ice accumulated in Leon Valley, New Braunfels, and San Geronimo. Icy roads were a problem across the region. Ice closed many roads across the region and caused numerous vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough crossed the Rockies with an associated 130 knot jet and lee side surface low over eastern Colorado.  The strongest winds occurred on the eastern slopes of the Franklin Mountains after sunset as a favorable temperature inversion setup for increased winds during westerly flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee near Cohen Stadium reported a peak gust of 68 mph at 9 PM.  An earlier peak gust of 65 mph occurred at 610 PM. The El Paso International Airport also reported a peak gust of 61 mph at 651 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third wintry precipitation event of a six-week period of repeated sharp cold snaps may have had the greatest impact on both Rio Grande Valley residents and agricultural interests. ||A strong cold front swept through the Ranchlands just before daybreak, and the rest of the Rio Grande Valley by around 8 AM, on January 16th. The strongest surface high pressure system of the winter quickly descended on the southern tip of Texas, with temperatures falling rapidly below freezing by mid to late morning across the Rio Grande Plains ranchlands, and into the 30s soon after noon elsewhere across the Valley. Drainage of the modified arctic air maximized across colder pockets of the more|populated Rio Grande Valley, and by the peak of the late afternoon/early evening commute, subfreezing temperatures had nosed into Weslaco, Elsa/Edcouch, Harlingen, and Raymondville. The 24 hour temperature difference from 4 PM January 15th to 4 PM January 16th ranged from 35 to 45��F; when adding the brutal northerly winds, the ���feels like��� temperature difference between the two days was up to a whopping 55|degrees colder (peaking in the Lower Valley between Brownsville and Raymondville)!||Accompanying the rapid temperature crash was spotty precipitation. Light freezing rain and a bit of sleet produced glazing across Hebbronville by mid to late morning, which continued into the early afternoon. Drier low level air arrive by late afternoon, and precipitation ceased across the Rio Grande Plains at or before sunset. Farther east, a pocket of evaporative cooling in the precipitation formation zone of the atmosphere|combined with just enough mid level energy to kick off a brief but surprising period of light snow (no impact) from Rio Grande City through the McAllen metropolitan area during the mid-afternoon, while surface temperatures were at or just above freezing. The snow (and some sleet) was conversational more than anything, and precipitation north and west of the populated Rio Grande Valley ended prior to notable impacts.||Such was not the case for much of the Rio Grande Valley���s population, along and near Interstate Highway (IH) 2, the south end of IH 69C (Edinburg to Pharr), and IH 69E (Brownsville to Harlingen). Light freezing drizzle developed prior to sunset as a nose of subfreezing temperatures slid southeast, with readings falling to between 28��F and 30��F from Weslaco to Harlingen and south toward north Brownsville. Though the|precipitation was light and for a shorter duration ��� 6 to 9 hours ��� than the nearly 24 hours of similar precipitation experienced during the February 3-4 2011 Ice Storm, impacts to elevated road surfaces and some power lines was both notable and dangerous. The early arrival of the glaze, in some locations prior to sunset|and during the end of the afternoon commute, caused vehicles to slide on the highways and would soon force closures of most elevated roadways (there is more than 80 miles in both directions) which continued through mid morning of the 17th before the ice would melt or evaporate. Unfortunately, more than two dozen accidents were reported on elevated highways prior to the closures, which diverted traffic onto frontage roads where ground temperatures were above freezing.||The combination of glaze ice and very cold temperatures, which led to statewide record electricity use (according to the Electric Reliability Council of Texas, or ERCOT), may have contributed to the more than 22,000 customers without power during the peak of the cold and icing, during the pre-dawn hours of January 17th. Numerous ���blue flashes��� of transformers were seen overnight from the 16th through the 17th before daybreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell to the freezing point around noon on January 16th, and dropped into the upper 20s by late afternoon then held through the night into the mid morning of the 17th before a slow rise above freezing with very low dew point temperatures (single numbers).  The cooperative location 3 miles west of Falfurrias fell to 27 overnight and early morning of the 17th. Duration of freezing temperatures ranged from 10 to more than 20 hours, with longest duration estimated to be in western portions of the county. |This particular event, coming on the heels of four consecutive freezing-point mornings to begin the New Year, likely was the final straw in full freeze-curing of rangelands for the remainder of winter.  Livestock impacts were unknown as of this writing.||Wind chill temperature fell into the upper teens during the peak of the combination of strong north wind and low temperatures during the afternoon and evening of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold arctic air mass moved into South Texas for New Year's Day. Sleet was observed in the northern Coastal Bend. This caused icy conditions for roadways. A seven vehicle crash occurred on an icy overpass in Live Oak County resulting in one fatality. An elderly man passed away due to exposure after he fell from his wheel chair in Corpus Christi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elderly man passed away due to exposure to the cold temperatures after he fell from his wheel chair in Corpus Christi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Aided in part by a moderate La Nina that developed in October 2017, a dry and seasonably mild string of months led to the emergence of D2 (severe) and D3 (extreme) drought conditions for the start of 2018.  The D2 drought encompassed all of the South Plains, Rolling Plains and the far southern TX Panhandle, with D3 drought focused in Cottle County.  Beginning January 12th, rainfall over the previous 110 days across the region was generally 10 to 30 percent of normal.  By January 22nd, Lubbock was already in its fourth longest stretch without measurable rainfall.  Areas south and east of Lubbock did receive measurable rainfall on January 7th and again on the 25th, however each episode was too minuscule to offset the prolonged dryness. Monetary impacts to agriculture and grazing were minimal at this time, with little stress dealt to area reservoirs.

Aided in part by a moderate La Nina that developed in October 2017, a dry and seasonably mild string of months led to the emergence of D2 (severe) and D3 (extreme) drought conditions for the start of 2018.  The D2 drought encompassed all of the South Plains, Rolling Plains and the far southern TX Panhandle, with D3 drought focused in Cottle County.  Beginning January 12th, rainfall over the previous 110 days across the region was generally 10 to 30 percent of normal.  By January 22nd, Lubbock was already in its fourth longest stretch without measurable rainfall.  Areas south and east of Lubbock did receive measurable rainfall on January 7th and again on the 25th, however each episode was too minuscule to offset the prolonged dryness. Monetary impacts to agriculture and grazing were minimal at this time, with little stress dealt to area reservoirs.

An incoming short wave provided ample lift of a very moist air mass to produce several hours of rainfall over Jefferson County. 8 to 12 inches of rain fell which caused flooding. A strong cold front then pushed through the area with north winds pushing the tides out for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS site KORG reported 4.76 during the heavy rain event over a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms occurred along a dryline and cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported a brief tornado near the intersection of Hopkins County Roads 2393 and 2399. Debris was encountered near the intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming short wave provided ample lift of a very moist air mass to produce several hours of rainfall over Jefferson County. 8 to 12 inches of rain fell which caused flooding. A strong cold front then pushed through the area with north winds pushing the tides out for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north winds pushed tides below normal for a few days with tides falling below -1 foot MLLW for 2 cycles. The tide fell to a minimum of -1.22 feet below MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Texas 'norther turned a cool December 31st into a brisk and cold New Year's Eve turn of the calendar, as temperatures crashed into the mid 30s to lower 40s across the ranches north of the populated Rio Grande Valley with wind chill values in the mid 20s to lower 30s with the turn of the year at midnight. Temperatures would continue to plunge, and by daybreak to an hour or two after daybreak reached their lowest points, ranging from the upper 20s in colder spots of the South Texas Brush Country to the lower and mid 30s across the Rio Grande Valley. The low temperatures were joined by very light precipitation, which fell in the form of light sleet and freezing drizzle near Hebbronville early on the 1st but with no known significant impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell steadily from the evening of December 31st through the morning of January 1st, reaching the freezing point (32) at Hebbronville/Jim Hogg County Airport a little after 230 AM, then falling further reaching 29 degrees just after 9 AM. Temperatures would fluctuate between 29 and 32 degrees through 10 AM on January 2nd. Light sleet and freezing drizzle joined the freezing temperatures at times between 230 and 1015 AM on the 1st, with minimal impact besides a light glaze on trees and grassy areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures, which only rose into the lower and mid 30s across much of the region on one of the coldest New Year's Days in years, slid back to or below the freezing point overnight and through the early to mid morning hours on January 2nd, 2018. Once again, light wintry precipitation created minor icing of trees and some grasses with minimal impact, this time in a pocket of the Lower Rio Grande Valley between Weslaco and Harlingen, extending north to Willacy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After only rising a few degrees into the lower and mid 30s during the afternoon of New Year's Day, temperatures would bottom out once again overnight and during the early morning of January 2nd, reaching 31 degrees 7 miles east of Sarita, and 30 degrees 4 miles southeast of Armstrong.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures, which only rose into the lower and mid 30s across much of the region on one of the coldest New Year's Days in years, slid back to or below the freezing point overnight and through the early to mid morning hours on January 2nd, 2018. EVENT_NARRATIVE: After only rising a few degrees into the mid 30s during the afternoon of New Year's Day, temperatures would bottom out at or below 32 degrees in much of inland Willacy County early on January 2nd. Low temperatures fell to 30 degrees at Raymondville, and proxy observations in eastern Hidalgo and southern Kenedy County suggested nearly all of the county had 32 degree or lower temperatures for several hours. It was not known if light icing had occurred, which was the case in western Cameron and eastern Hidalgo County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures, which only rose into the lower and mid 30s across much of the region on one of the coldest New Year's Days in years, slid back to or below the freezing point overnight and through the early to mid morning hours on January 2nd, 2018. EVENT_NARRATIVE: After only rising a few degrees into the mid and upper 30s during the afternoon of New Year's Day, temperatures would bottom out around 32 degrees across western Cameron County early on January 2nd. Low temperatures fell to 32 degrees in Harlingen, and there were several reports of light icing on trees and grasses with limited impact.  Temperatures would struggle to rise for a second day, reaching the upper 30s in most areas and setting the stage for another freezing morning on the 3rd.

Weslaco/Mid Valley Airport fell to 31 degrees at around 830 AM; at this time, light freezing drizzle and perhaps some sleet resulted in a few reports of light icing on trees and grasses with limited impact.  Temperatures would struggle to rise for a second day, reaching the upper 30s in most areas and setting the stage for another freezing morning on the 3rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: After only rising a few degrees into the low to mid 30s during the afternoon of New Year's Day, temperatures would bottom out around or just below 32 degrees across much of Starr County. Falcon Lake Mesowest would bottom out at 32 degrees shortly after 2 AM and continue at or near the freezing point through 730-8 AM. The cooperative observer in Rio Grande City recorded a minimum temperature of 30 degrees.  There did not appear to be any notable freezing precipitation in Starr on the 2nd, where drier air was present near the surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most counties remained drought-free in January, but a few locations west of the I-35 corridor dropped back into severe drought by the end of the month due to below-normal rainfall across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe/D2 drought conditions developed across Coryell County during the last week of January 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most counties remained drought-free in January, but a few locations west of the I-35 corridor dropped back into severe drought by the end of the month due to below-normal rainfall across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe/D2 drought conditions developed across Wise County during the last week of January 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a second day where temperatures struggled to escape the 30s, and wind chill temperatures held in the 20s for much of the day in most areas, overnight temperatures slid back to or below the freezing point (32 degrees) across much of the region during the late night through the early morning hours of January 3rd. Unlike the prior two mornings, where pockets of light drizzle fell with the freezing temperatures to create conversational glaze ice, January 3rd was precipitation free, after light precipitation fell in the form of a cold light rain during the evening of the 2nd. ||Sunshine finally arrived to most areas on the 3rd, allowing temperatures to rise into the lower 50s by afternoon.  With lighter north winds and dry low levels, the stage was set for the coldest morning, area wide, on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observation station 3 miles west of Falfurrias recorded a low of 31 degrees toward daybreak on January 3rd. Based on proxy observations in western Kenedy County and northern Hidalgo County, low temperatures in some ranch areas likely dropped a bit lower.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observation stations 7 miles east of Sarita and 4 miles southeast of Armstrong recorded minimum temperatures of 29 and 28 degrees, respectively, shortly after daybreak on January 3rd.  Kingsville Naval Air Station, about 22 miles north of Sarita, dipped to 26 degrees around 8 AM before the sun finally brought a relative warming trend. Times were estimated based on the Kingsville observations and those from northern Hidalgo County.

Proxy observations at Port Mansfield (coastal Willacy, at 31 degrees) and San Manuel (northeast Hidalgo), as well as Armstrong, Kenedy County (28 degrees) made the case for all of the county to have reached 32 degrees or lower for at least two hours.

With the exception of the Rio Grande, county locations reached 32 degrees or slightly lower for a couple hours early on the 3rd, mainly between 6 and 8 AM.

Low temperatures bottomed out at 27 degrees, with proxy data farther south suggesting upper 20s to around 30 for minimum values. Duration of freezing temperatures likely ranged from 4 to 12 hours, with longest duration across the northern tier where the density of population is largest.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS station at Zapata County Airport dipped to 32 for six hours between 3 and 9 AM on the 3rd. Based on proxy observations in Jim Hogg County, eastern sections of  Zapata may have fallen a degree or two lower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds allowed temperatures to fall steadily after sunrise from January 3rd highs in the lower 50s, and soon after midnight readings were at or below 32 across the ranchlands and wind-protected areas of the upper Rio Grande Valley.  Low temperatures bottomed out in the mid to upper 20s across most of the ranchlands, with slightly higher temperatures (still below 32) in non-wind-protected locations along Texas State Route 285. Frost was common in the Lower Valley and may well have occurred in wind protected colder locations elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Falfurrias recorded a low temperature of 31 degrees.  However, wind protected ranchlands south and southwest likely fell into the mid and upper 20s, based on proxy observations from Kenedy, nothern Hidalgo, and western Willacy County. While frost was not recorded specifically, wind protected locations may have received some due to the clear, calm conditions that followed several days of cloudy and chilly conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds allowed temperatures to fall steadily after sunrise from January 3rd highs in the lower 50s, and soon after midnight readings were at or below 32 across the ranchlands and wind-protected areas of the upper Rio Grande Valley.  Low temperatures bottomed out in the mid to upper 20s across most of the ranchlands, with slightly higher temperatures (still below 32) in non-wind-protected locations along Texas State Route 285. Frost was common in the Lower Valley and may well have occurred in wind protected colder locations elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Brownsville/South Padre Island International Airport fell to 31 degrees early on the 4th, the coldest temperature recorded there since nearly a year ago (January 7, 2017, also 31 degrees).  Harlingen/Valley International Airport fell to 30 degrees, and a few locations may have touched into the upper 20s.  Light frost was fairly widespread across the county. Impact to agriculture from this particular day was unknown; multiple cold days in a row followed by another long-duration but minor freeze less than two weeks later may have aggregated the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold arctic air mass moved into South Texas for New Year's Day. Sleet was observed in the northern Coastal Bend. This caused icy conditions for roadways. A seven vehicle crash occurred on an icy overpass in Live Oak County resulting in one fatality. An elderly man passed away due to exposure after he fell from his wheel chair in Corpus Christi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A seven vehicle accident occurred near an icy overpass on Interstate 37 at Highway 59 in Live Oak County. A 21 year old man was killed in the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low to mid level winds at the base of a potent upper trough passing over the region  imparted strong winds to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong west winds were reported near Guadalupe Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate wind gusts to 61 mph were measured in the far southeast Texas Panhandle early this morning following a strong cold front.  No damage was reported.
Two separate wind gusts to 61 mph were measured in the far southeast Texas Panhandle early this morning following a strong cold front.  No damage was reported.
A cold front pushed through Southeast Texas and into a humid and unstable air mass. Showers and storms developed with one tornado occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado briefly touched down on Country Road 451, destroying one barn and damaging the roof of another barn. The path length was around half a mile. Several trees had the tops twisted off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of January 25th, a grass fire developed in rural southeast Motley County near the Pease River and Highway 62.  Over the course of the next six days, the fire spread northeast and consumed approximately 5942 acres before 100% containment was achieved.  Fortunately, no structures were lost.
Dry and windy conditions behind a cold front led to elevated fire danger on Monday January 22, resulting in several grass fires across North Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Denton Fire and Rescue reported a 200 acre wildfire near the Highway 377 and Cross Timbers intersection in the city of Roanoke, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and windy conditions behind a cold front led to elevated fire danger on Monday January 22, resulting in several grass fires across North Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire and Rescue reported a wildfire of 14.5 acres just east of Highway 377 between the cities of Gordonville and Sandusky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and windy conditions behind a cold front led to elevated fire danger on Monday January 22, resulting in several grass fires across North Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Leon County Emergency Management reported a wildfire of 28 acres approximately 6 miles southwest of the city of Oakwood, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and windy conditions behind a cold front led to elevated fire danger on Monday January 22, resulting in several grass fires across North Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire and Rescue reported a wildfire of 35 acres approximately 4 miles southeast of the city of Palestine, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and windy conditions behind a cold front led to elevated fire danger on Monday January 22, resulting in several grass fires across North Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire and rescue reported that 200 acres had burned approximately 4 miles southeast of the city of Alvord, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough moved out of the Inter Mountain West and into the Southern Plains on January 21st. This trough allowed for the return of much warmer temperatures and a return of Gulf of Mexico moisture, replacing a cold continental arctic air mass the Southern Plains had been under the influence of the week before. By afternoon, dewpoints were in the lower 60s as far north as the Middle Red River Valley of Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas. This yielded CAPE values around 500 J/KG but very strong Deep Layer Shear was present along with strong low level directional shear. Storms ignited during the late afternoon and early evening hours of January 21st across Northern Texas and Southern Oklahoma along and just ahead of a dryline. Later in the evening, low level directional shear continued to increase and therefore, storms took on the form of supercells. Some of these storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts. One long track supercell resulted in a tornado which did damage across portions of Bowie County Texas before crossing the Red River and continuing into Little River County Arkansas. The Pacific cold front in association with the upper level trough eventually caught up with the dryline moving into Northeast Texas and Southeast Oklahoma during the overnight hours of January 21st and into the predawn hours of January 22nd, but this broken line of thunderstorms was not responsible for any additional wind damage, hail, or tornadoes across Northeast Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball sized hail fell in Yantis.

The Pacific cold front in association with the upper level trough eventually caught up with the dryline moving into Northeast Texas and Southeast Oklahoma during the overnight hours of January 21st and into the predawn hours of January 22nd, but this broken line of thunderstorms was not responsible for any additional wind damage, hail, or tornadoes across Northeast Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The top of a large pine tree snapped and fell onto a one-story home on Winn Drive near West Broadway Street, causing significant roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough moved out of the Inter Mountain West and into the Southern Plains on January 21st. This trough allowed for the return of much warmer temperatures and a return of Gulf of Mexico moisture, replacing a cold continental arctic air mass the Southern Plains had been under the influence of the week before. By afternoon, dewpoints were in the lower 60s as far north as the Middle Red River Valley of Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas. This yielded CAPE values around 500 J/KG but very strong Deep Layer Shear was present along with strong low level directional shear. Storms ignited during the late afternoon and early evening hours of January 21st across Northern Texas and Southern Oklahoma along and just ahead of a dryline. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball size hail fell in Mount Pleasant.

The tornado also uprooted several hardwood trees before dissipating along Highway 992.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved across the Texas Panhandle during the afternoon hours of the 21st. A very strong height gradient from this system spread over the South Plains region. As a result, widespread strong winds of 30 to 40 mph were reported area wide, with some gusts over 50 mph at times. A Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet site near O'Donnell (Lynn County) measured a wind gust to 59 mph.
Dry and windy conditions behind a cold front led to elevated fire danger on Monday January 22, resulting in several grass fires across North Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported that 1,000 acres had burned near Willow Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across the Corpus Christi metro area on January 27th. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches in a couple of hours produced flooding of roadways in the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roadways across the city of Corpus Christi were flooded due to heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire in northern Victoria County burned around 800 acres during the afternoon of the 29th. The wildfire occurred near Nursery Road and Garrett Gonzalez School Road near the community of Fordtran.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire burned around 800 acres in northern Victoria County near Nursery Road and Garrett Gonzalez School Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought a shallow layer of subfreezing air to South Central Texas. Isentropic lift of warm moist air over this shallow cold layer led to wintry precipitation.  Most of the precipitation was freezing rain and sleet, but there was some snow toward the end of the event. There were reports of 1/8 inch of ice accumulated in Leon Valley, New Braunfels, and San Geronimo. Icy roads were a problem across the region. Ice closed many roads across the region and caused numerous vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought a shallow layer of subfreezing air to South Central Texas. Isentropic lift of warm moist air over this shallow cold layer led to wintry precipitation.  Most of the precipitation was freezing rain and sleet, but there was some snow toward the end of the event. There were reports of 1/8 inch of ice accumulated in Leon Valley, New Braunfels, and San Geronimo. Icy roads were a problem across the region. Ice closed many roads across the region and caused numerous vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming short wave provided ample lift of a very moist air mass to produce several hours of rainfall over Jefferson County. 8 to 12 inches of rain fell which caused flooding. A strong cold front then pushed through the area with north winds pushing the tides out for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures on social media indicated standing water and stalled cars along Jimmy Johnson Blvd shortly after 6 AM. Heavy rain continued with one rain gauge reporting 10.18 inches in Groves and another reporting 7.68 inches in Hampshire. Many streets were flooded during the event with numerous homes becoming flooded in Port Acres where some were being remodeled from flooding due to Harvey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure spread east across the Southern Plains during the evening hours of January 15th, with a arctic air mass quickly spilling south into the Ark-La-Tex during the late afternoon through the evening hours behind a strong cold front. Scattered areas of light rain developed along and just behind the front over much of Northeast Texas during the early evening, with the rain quickly changing over to a mixture of freezing rain, sleet, and then snow during the overnight hours. Ice accumulations of up to a tenth of an inch were common across the western sections of Northeast Texas, with snowfall accumulations of one to two inches, with isolated higher amounts observed. This caused significant travel impacts across much of Northeast Texas, with the rain and melted snow quickly freezing into black ice on many roadways, bridges and overpasses, as temperatures plummeted into the upper teens to lower 20s by daybreak on the morning of the 16th. ||Here are some of the snowfall totals across Northeast Texas: In Franklin County, 0.1 inches of snow was recorded in Mount Vernon. An arctic cold front passed through the area during the evening of the 15th. The cold air mass filtered in during the night and light rain transitioned over to snow as temperatures fell below freezing. Accumulations were less than an inch, however icy spots closed bridges across the area which produced travel problems. Strong north winds from a front on the 12th and again on the 16th produce low tides along the coast for 6 cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light dusting of snow over a thin glaze of ice occurred during the morning of the 16th. Area travel was interrupted and area schools canceled classes for the day. Local media obtained a video showing a dusting on mainly elevated and grassy surfaces by 10 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds allowed temperatures to fall steadily after sunrise from January 3rd highs in the lower 50s, and soon after midnight readings were at or below 32 across the ranchlands and wind-protected areas of the upper Rio Grande Valley.  Low temperatures bottomed out in the mid to upper 20s across most of the ranchlands, with slightly higher temperatures (still below 32) in non-wind-protected locations along Texas State Route 285. Frost was common in the Lower Valley and may well have occurred in wind protected colder locations elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures at Rio Grande City and Falcon Lake RAWS fell to 27 and 26 degrees respectively, the coldest since the same readings were recorded nearly a year earlier (January 8, 2017).  By proxy, much of Starr County likely fell to the upper 20s based on observations from Zapata to Jim Hogg and western Hidalgo County. Clear, calm  conditions aided the steady drop in temperatures after sunset on the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Texas 'norther turned a cool December 31st into a brisk and cold New Year's Eve turn of the calendar, as temperatures crashed into the mid 30s to lower 40s across the ranches north of the populated Rio Grande Valley with wind chill values in the mid 20s to lower 30s with the turn of the year at midnight. Temperatures would continue to plunge, and by daybreak to an hour or two after daybreak reached their lowest points, ranging from the upper 20s in colder spots of the South Texas Brush Country to the lower and mid 30s across the Rio Grande Valley. The low temperatures were joined by very light precipitation, which fell in the form of light sleet and freezing drizzle near Hebbronville early on the 1st but with no known significant impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers 7 miles east of Sarita and 4 miles southeast of Armstrong recorded low temperatures of 32 and 31 degrees, respectively, for an estimated 2 hours early on New Year's Day 2018.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell steadily from the evening of December 31st through the morning of January 1st, reaching the freezing point (32) at Zapata County Airport just before 7 AM, and holding right around the freezing point through 1035 AM on January 2nd.  Light sleet and freezing drizzle joined the freezing temperatures at times between between 7 AM and 415 PM, most likely across the northern half of the county but with impact only to grassy areas and some brush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds allowed temperatures to fall steadily after sunrise from January 3rd highs in the lower 50s, and soon after midnight readings were at or below 32 across the ranchlands and wind-protected areas of the upper Rio Grande Valley.  Low temperatures bottomed out in the mid to upper 20s across most of the ranchlands, with slightly higher temperatures (still below 32) in non-wind-protected locations along Texas State Route 285. Frost was common in the Lower Valley and may well have occurred in wind protected colder locations elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer 7 miles east of Sarita recorded 23 degrees, their coldest temperatures since January 7 and 8 (26 and 27) 2017 under clear skies and calm winds. Other nearby observations included 26 in Kingsville and San Manuel (northeast Hidalgo), and 28 in Raymondville. Duration of subfreezing temperatures is estimated from proxy observations at the RAWS/Mesowest location in San Manuel, adjusted to include both wind and non-wind protected locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer in Raymondville recorded a low temperature of 28 degrees.  Temperatures across the county were likely in the upper 20s, based on proxy observations at Linn/San Manuel RAWS (26) and Harlingen/Valley Int'l Airport ASOS (30). Duration of event was a blend between the colder ranching communities and slightly warmer farming communities closer to the more populated Lower Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds allowed temperatures to fall steadily after sunrise from January 3rd highs in the lower 50s, and soon after midnight readings were at or below 32 across the ranchlands and wind-protected areas of the upper Rio Grande Valley. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer in Port Mansfield recorded a low temperature of 32 degrees under clear and mainly calm conditions. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure spread east across the Southern Plains during the evening hours of January 15th, with a arctic air mass quickly spilling south into the Ark-La-Tex during the late afternoon through the evening hours behind a strong cold front. Scattered areas of light rain developed along and just behind the front over much of Northeast Texas during the early evening, with the rain quickly changing over to a mixture of freezing rain, sleet, and then snow during the overnight hours. Ice accumulations of up to a tenth of an inch were common across the western sections of Northeast Texas, with snowfall accumulations of one to two inches, with isolated higher amounts observed. This caused significant travel impacts across much of Northeast Texas, with the rain and melted snow quickly freezing into black ice on many roadways, bridges and overpasses, as temperatures plummeted into the upper teens to lower 20s by daybreak on the morning of the 16th. ||Here are some of the snowfall totals across Northeast Texas: In Franklin County, 0.1 inches of snow was recorded in Mount Vernon. In Titus County, Mount Pleasant recorded 1.0 inches of snow. In Camp County, Pittsburg recorded 2.0 inches of snow. In Morris County, Lone Star recorded 3.0 inches, and Daingerfield recorded 2.5 inches of snow. In Bowie County, 2 miles east of Leary recorded 3.0 inches of snow, 1 mile north northeast of Texarkana recorded 3.0 inches, 1 mile southeast of New Boston recorded 1.1 inches, New Boston also recorded 1.1 inches, and Dekalb recorded 0.7 inches of snow. In Cass County, Hughes Springs recorded 3.4 inches, Atlanta 3.0 inches, and 3 miles south southwest of Atlanta 2.5 inches. In Marion County, 7 miles south of Avinger recorded 2.8 inches of snow, and Jefferson 2.0 inches. In Harrison County, Scottsville recorded 2.1 inches of snow, 6 miles south of Scottsville 1.8 inches, Jonesville 1.5 inches, 3 miles west northwest of Waskom 1.5 inches, and Hallsville 5.5 miles north 1.5 inches.  In Upshur County, 1 mile west northwest of Union Grive recorded 2.0 inches of snow, 7 miles south southwest of Ore City 2.0 inches, 6 miles north of Big Sandy 1.8 inches, and Gilmer 1.8 inches. In Gregg County, 2 miles northwest of Longview recorded 2.5 inches of snow, 2 miles east southeast of Longview 2.0 inches, 3 miles east north of Longview 2.0 inches, 4 miles north of Longview 2.0 inches, 2 miles east of  Longview 1.5 inches, and 3 miles east southeast of Longview 1.5 inches. In Wood County, Mineola recorded 2.5 inches of snow, and 2 miles east of Mineola recorded 1.5 inches. In Smith County, 3 miles west of Tyler recorded 2.0 inches of snow, 3 miles west of Lindale 2.0 inches, 2 miles east southeast of Tyler 2.0 inches, 1 mile south of Tyler 2.0 inches, 2 miles south of Tyler 1.5 inches, 1 mile north northeast of Tyler 1.5 inches, 1 mile south southwest of Tyler 1.3 inches, 2 miles south of Tyler 1.3 inches, Bullard 1.0 inches, and 1 mile west southwest of Tyler 1.0 inches. In Cherokee County, 1 mile south of Bullard recorded 1.0 inches of snow, and Jacksonville recorded 0.2 inches. In Rusk County, 5 miles south southeast of New London recorded 1.2 inches of snow, 2 miles south of Kilgore 1.1 inches, 1 mile south southwest of Kilgore 1.1 inches, and Henderson 1.0 inches. In Panola County, Carthage recorded 2.0 inches of snow, and Beckville also recorded 2.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds allowed temperatures to fall steadily after sunrise from January 3rd highs in the lower 50s, and soon after midnight readings were at or below 32 across the ranchlands and wind-protected areas of the upper Rio Grande Valley.  Low temperatures bottomed out in the mid to upper 20s across most of the ranchlands, with slightly higher temperatures (still below 32) in non-wind-protected locations along Texas State Route 285. Frost was common in the Lower Valley and may well have occurred in wind protected colder locations elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS/Mesowest location at Laguna Atascosa National Wildlife Refuge fell to 29 degrees, and Bayview/Cameron County Airport fell to 31 degrees, under clear and calm conditions early on January 4th. cooperative observer in Port Mansfield recorded a low temperature of 32 degrees under clear and mainly calm conditions.  These were the coldest temperatures along the Lower Texas coast since January 7, 2017 - nearly one year earlier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds allowed temperatures to fall steadily after sunrise from January 3rd highs in the lower 50s, and soon after midnight readings were at or below 32 across the ranchlands and wind-protected areas of the upper Rio Grande Valley.  Low temperatures bottomed out in the mid to upper 20s across most of the ranchlands, with slightly higher temperatures (still below 32) in non-wind-protected locations along Texas State Route 285. Frost was common in the Lower Valley and may well have occurred in wind protected colder locations elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of Hidalgo County fell to or below freezing early on January 4th, with the sole exception of the City of McAllen. Across the ranch and farming communities north of the populated areas, temperatures fell into the mid and upper 20s, as did wind-protected Santa Ana National Wildlife Refuge on the Rio Grande near Pharr (23 degrees). Weslaco and Edinburg fell to 32.  In general for the county, about 4 hours of freezing temperatures were observed under clear and calm conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough moved out of the Inter Mountain West and into the Southern Plains on January 21st. This trough allowed for the return of much warmer temperatures and a return of Gulf of Mexico moisture, replacing a cold continental arctic air mass the Southern Plains had been under the influence of the week before. By afternoon, dewpoints were in the lower 60s as far north as the Middle Red River Valley of Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas. This yielded CAPE values around 500 J/KG but very strong Deep Layer Shear was present along with strong low level directional shear. Storms ignited during the late afternoon and early evening hours of January 21st across Northern Texas and Southern Oklahoma along and just ahead of a dryline. Later in the evening, low level directional shear continued to increase and therefore, storms took on the form of supercells. Some of these storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts. One long track supercell resulted in a tornado which did damage across portions of Bowie County Texas before crossing the Red River and continuing into Little River County Arkansas. The Pacific cold front in association with the upper level trough eventually caught up with the dryline moving into Northeast Texas and Southeast Oklahoma during the overnight hours of January 21st and into the predawn hours of January 22nd, but this broken line of thunderstorms was not responsible for any additional wind damage, hail, or tornadoes across Northeast Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado with maximum estimated winds around 125 mph touched down along Highway 259 just southwest of Dekalb and tracked northeast across the intersection of County Road 4231 and Farm to Market Road 44. Here, it caused significant damage to a well constructed two-story home shifting it off its foundation, and significantly damaging eight chicken houses, two of which were destroyed. The tornadic storm continued northeast where it crossed County Road 4230 and significantly damaged the Dekalb Water Treatment Plant, before crossing the intersection of Farm to Market Road 990 and Farm to Market Road 1840 where a one-story home had its roof removed and several hardwood and softwood trees were snapped or uprooted. As the tornado tracked across County Road 4228, there were signs of it widening where the snapped hardwood trees and downed power lines were more noticeable. Once the tornado tracked across Highway 82, hardwood trees snapped at its base and significant damage to roofs of a church and nearby one and two-story homes were prevalent. The tornadic storm continued to move northeast across County Road 3201 where numerous hardwood trees were downed and a camper was destroyed. The tornado continued northeast where it crossed County Road 3203, uplifting a metal roof from a home and destroyed seven chicken farm buildings. Once it crossed northeast across County Road 3202, it split and twisted hardwood trees and destroyed an outbuilding before finally dissipating.||In all, 16 single family homes were damaged, 9 suffering minor damage, and 7 with major damage, with 32 mobile homes damaged, 25 suffering minor damage, 4 with major damage, and 3 mobile homes destroyed. The Dekalb Water Treatment Plant suffered about $500,000 in damage, with another $500,000 damage done to the  chicken houses that were destroyed, as well to an auto repair shop and a gun shop that were significantly damaged. Approximately 200,000 chickens were killed, and 1 cow was killed as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures at Zapata County Airport fell to 26 degrees, as did the low at Falcon Lake RAWS, just inside Starr County south of the Zapata/Starr County line. Based on observations in Starr County, low temperatures likely fell into the mid and upper 20s county-wide under clear and calm conditions.

Low temperatures bottomed out in the mid to upper 20s across most of the ranchlands, with slightly higher temperatures (still below 32) in non-wind-protected locations along Texas State Route 285. Frost was common in the Lower Valley and may well have occurred in wind protected colder locations elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures at Jim Hogg County Airport 4 miles northwest of Hebbronville reached 30 degrees early on January 4th, but a pocket of mid to upper 20s temperatures likely was observed across most ranch areas based on proxy observations in Hidalgo, Starr, and Zapata County. Clear, calm conditions led to the coldest readings since January 8, 2017.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blast of very dry air, fed by strong northwest flow at all levels of the atmosphere that originated from the Pacific, rather than the North Pole, surged through the southern Rockies and Sierra Madre Oriental on its way to south Texas and the Rio Grande Valley on January 11th, 2018. Downslope flow during the afternoon of the 11th pushed temperatures well into the 80s, but dropped humidity into the teens as northwest winds picked up to 15 to 25 mph, on average.||The true front arrived by mid to late afternoon across the Rio Grande Plains and South Texas Brush Country ranchlands, then surged into the Rio Grande Valley from sunset through 11 PM. Very strong winds, as high as 60 mph just above the surface, slammed into the region, creating scattered power outages, a number of grass fires, and localized wind damage to trees, power poles, and some roofs. Downed power lines caused numerous small grass fires between Edinburg and Mission, with additional small trees/limbs down in Combes (near Harlingen). At Brownsville/South Padre Island International Airport, a blown down line sparked a small wildfire just down the road from the NWS office. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with frequent gusts ranging|from 40 to nearly 55 mph lasted for about three hours in most inland locations, but up to nine hours along the Lower Texas coast, where the moderate gale persisted well into the night of the 11th and into the pre-dawn hours of the 12th|. |Preliminary power outage statistics across the Valley suggested between 3,000 and 5,000 customers lost power during the peak of the gusts, based on preliminary reports from local power providers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Management Coordinator of Zapata County, Texas, reported several large limbs blown down near a house in the city of Zapata, and the canopy of a gas station along US 83 was flipped off its moorings, causing minor damage to vehicles underneath but no injuries.  Peak gusts were estimated at 50 mph; the nearby Zapata County Airport which is a bit wind-protected, reached 40 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gusts ranged generally from 39 to 54 mph (peak 11 north of Edinburg at the South Texas International Airport) during the mid evening of January 11. Numerous small structural fires and other small grass fires were reported by Edinburg Fire, and multiple power lines were reported downed in Mission from the same post-frontal surge of northwest winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A surge of strong winds behind the dry front late on January 11th produced peak gusts between 44 and 54 mph during the mid evening.  Peak gusts of 53 mph at Harlingen/Valley International Airport (903 PM CT) and 54 mph at Bayview/Cameron County Airport (739 PM) partially contributed to patchy minor damage, including a tree down in Combes; the gusty winds at Brownsville/South Padre Island International Airport (50 mph at 840 PM) sparked a brief fire on the airport grounds when a transformer blew near Iowa Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Aided in part by a moderate La Nina that developed in October 2017, a dry and seasonably mild string of months led to the emergence of D2 (severe) and D3 (extreme) drought conditions for the start of 2018.  The D2 drought encompassed all of the South Plains, Rolling Plains and the far southern TX Panhandle, with D3 drought focused in Cottle County.  Beginning January 12th, rainfall over the previous 110 days across the region was generally 10 to 30 percent of normal.  By January 22nd, Lubbock was already in its fourth longest stretch without measurable rainfall.  Areas south and east of Lubbock did receive measurable rainfall on January 7th and again on the 25th, however each episode was too minuscule to offset the prolonged dryness. Monetary impacts to agriculture and grazing were minimal at this time, with little stress dealt to area reservoirs.

An upper level low pressure system progressed east southeast from the Pacific Northwest into the Central and Southern Rockies on the morning of February 20th, reinforcing a cold front slowly southeast into Southeast Oklahoma, Northeast Texas, and Southwest Arkansas during the early morning hours of February 21st. A deep southwest flow aloft was present along and behind the front across the Southern Plains and Mississippi Valley, with weak embedded disturbances in the flow helping to enhance the development of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms along and behind the front. A warm, moist, and weakly unstable air mass spread north ahead of the front into Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, and North Louisiana, with the southwest flow aloft tapping into the subtropical jet, thus transporting Pacific moisture northeast along and behind the front. Periods of moderate to at times, heavy rainfall fell along and behind the front across Northeast Texas during the evening hours on the 20th through much of the 21st before gradually diminishing during the late evening hours. However, additional periods of moderate to heavy rain spread across much of East Texas on February 22nd north of the cold front, and continued through the morning hours on the 23rd. Widespread rainfall amounts of four to in excess of nine inches fell across much of East Texas during this 3 day period, resulting in widespread flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water flowing over FM 1804 along the Wood/Smith County line due to backwater flooding on the Sabine River from periods of moderate to heavy rainfall across the Sabine River Basin. Road is closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a warm, very moist airmass over South Central Texas and generated thunderstorms. Some of these storms produced large hail and heavy rain. The heavy rain led to localized flash flooding. Rainfall totals in worst hit counties ranged from 4.09 to 5.72 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the early morning hours, a cluster of storms moved east across portions of Caldwell County. Along the leading edge of the thunderstorms, an NWS survey concluded a small EF-0 (estimated peak wind, 80mph) tornado formed and produced some minor damage approximately seven miles northeast of Luling. Wind damage to some trees was found on CR 144 with some roof damage reported off of Wattsville Rd. A small tornado track was found that crossed Pasture Rd. and continued east just north of Wattsville Rd. ending just east of Tenney Creek Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic West Texas wind storm occurred on the afternoon of the 18th and lasted into the early morning hours of the 19th. A very tight but potent upper level short wave moved out of the Great Basin into the four corners and then into northern New Mexico on the 18th. This allowed extremely a strong belt of mid level winds to spread across the South Plains and southern Texas Panhandle. High winds were mostly observed in the western South Plains from about 1600 CST to 2000 CST. A secondary wind maximum developed across the central South Plains into the western Rolling Plains from about 2200 CST on the 18th to 0200 CST on the 19th. Wildfire activity was exacerbated by the lack of significant rainfall in several months. Several wildfires were reported throughout the day and evening hours but none destroyed any structures or grew to more than a few hundred acres before being contained.||High wind gusts below were observed by Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet sites:||69 mph at Earth (Lamb County), 61 mph at Aiken (Hale County), 61 mph at Spur (Dickens County), 60 mph at Olton (Lamb County) and Roaring Springs (Motley County), 59 mph at Slaton (Lubbock County), Abernathy (Hale County), Lubbock International Airport ASOS (Lubbock County), and Guthrie (King County), 58 mph at Friona (Parmer County), Hart (Castro County), Anton (Hockley County), Happy (Swisher County), Tulia (Swisher County), Vigo Park (Swisher County), Morton (Cochran County), and Ralls (Crosby County).
