EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary moving east across the western Panhandles generated severe wind gusts with some damage reported as well. A lone thunderstorm that developed over Canyon moved north out ahead of the outflow boundary. Once the thunderstorm interacted with the boundary, strong vertical growth in the thunderstorm helped to developed up to golf ball sized hail in parts of south Amarillo. Thunderstorm then moved north over parts of western Amarillo before the lift from the boundary moved east of the city and weakened the thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Schoolnet report. Also there were reports of tree limbs and power poles down in Bushland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak steering wind flow, high instability and low to moderate wind shear allowed a cluster of thunderstorms to develop across the northeastern TX Panhandle. With very weak flow, thunderstorms were very slow to move and caused flash flooding across portions of the aforementioned region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cars flooded out due to running water over roadways in N Perryton. Three-quarters of a mile of us highway 83 covered with flowing water, and water making contact with Wright elementary school.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: State and local emergency management officials, as well as ground truth rainfall totals, reported widespread flooding in south-central Hidalgo County, focused on the Mission/Alton/West McAllen area as a firehose of thunderstorms persisted for more than five hours through the morning commute until around noon before finally dissipating. Estimated and measured rainfall for the event ranged from 6 to 11 inches, with periods of 2 to 3 inches per hour embedded during the heart of the morning commute.  Numerous roads, thousands of homes and vehicles (specific number unknown) were under a range of water depth, ranging from 18 inches to 3 to 4 feet in either poorly draining neighborhoods from the west half of McAllen through Mission.||A peak total rainfall of 18.31 inches fell in Mission for the combined events of June 20-22.  Estimated damage, using a combination of county-reported damage and standard doubling, was an estimated $22 million for this particular event.  Actual values may be much higher as data continues to come in throughout 2019.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed during the first week of August across much of Camp, Upshur, Gregg, and Cass Counties in Northeast Texas, as much above normal temperatures and below normal rainfall was observed throughout July, carrying over into August. July monthly rainfall totals ranged from an inch and a half to two inches across much of these areas, with isolated higher amounts in excess of three inches noted. This dryness was only exasperated by the multiple days of 100+ degree temperatures recorded between July 16th-26th, with temperatures exceeding 105 degrees on the 19th-22nd. As a result of the (near) record heat and dryness, burn bans were in effect for the latter half of July, and continued throughout August for these areas. After a temporary reprieve from severe drought across Camp and Upshur Counties during Mid-July, a second period of severe drought again affected and continued over these areas throughout August, as monthly rainfall amounts only ranged from a half inch to around two inches, some one to two inches below normal. With much of the month also experiencing temperatures in the upper 90s to near 100 degrees, extreme drought conditions (D3) were depicted across Western Cass, Eastern Camp, and Eastern Upshur Counties throughout much of August, beginning with the August 9th issuance of the U.S. Drought Monitor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The 602018 Wildfire began around 1300CST about seven miles east northeast of Kelton Texas in Wheeler county. The wildfire started just west of the Texas and Oklahoma state line or just west of North County Road 1670 about a half mile north of State Highway 152. The wildfire consumed an estimated three hundred acres, but there were no homes or other structures threatened by the wildfire and there were no homes or other structures destroyed by the wildfire. There also no reports of any injuries or fatalities.  The Wheeler Volunteer Fire Department, the Sweetwater Fire Department, the Kelton Volunteer Fire Department and the Allison Volunteer Fire Department all responded to the wildfire. The cause of the wildfire was determined to be from welding equipment use and the wildfire was both contained and controlled around 1600CST.

The 0000118 Wildfire began about eleven miles west of Hartley Texas in Hartley County around 1742CST. The wildfire consumed four hundred and fifty-one acres and there were no reports of any homes or other structures either threatened or destroyed by the wildfire. There were also no reports of any injuries or fatalities. The wildfire was controlled around 2032CST.

Extreme (D3) and severe (D2) drought conditions continued across Franklin, Titus, Camp, Morris, Marion, and Harrison Counties in Northeast Texas throughout the entire month of August, with continued below normal rainfall which ranged from three-quarters of an inch to around two inches across these areas. These monthly amounts were near to up to two inches below normal, with temperatures climbing into the upper 90s to near 100 degrees throughout much of the month, only exasperating drought conditions across these areas. By the second week of the month, extreme drought had spread into much of Franklin, Morris, Eastern Camp, and Western Marion Counties, which lingered into September.

Severe drought conditions across the Red River counties and areas along and east of the I-35 corridor improved to moderate drought status by the middle of August following beneficial rainfall which fell during the second week of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions improved to Moderate (D1) drought by the middle of August following significant rainfall during the second week of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a surface trough located between the Red River and the Interstate 20 corridor. Several of these storms ended up producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported multiple large trees down and damage to Sherrill Park Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a surface trough located between the Red River and the Interstate 20 corridor. Several of these storms ended up producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire in the attic of a home in the city of Frisco, TX. The homeowner was able to contain the fire before the fire department arrived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a surface trough located between the Red River and the Interstate 20 corridor. Several of these storms ended up producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio reported that 2-inch diameter tree limbs and power lines were blown onto the road near the intersection of Independence Parkway and Maumalle Drive in the city of Plano, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms originating in Oklahoma and Northwest Texas worked their way into the northwest counties of North Texas, bringing gusty thunderstorm winds to the area during the afternoon and evening hours of August 17, 2018.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pieces of a mobile home roof were removed by gusty winds in the city of Caps Corners, TX as storms moved in from the northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front provided focus for thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours Thursday through Saturday. Most of these storms remained below severe limits, but a few storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frisco Fire Department determined that a house fire was caused by lightning from a Thursday evening thunderstorm. The fire rendered the building unsafe and generated approximately 400,000 dollars in damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was an upper trough over West Texas that extended from northern Mexico northeast to the Great Lakes region. A couple of remaining thunderstorm outflow boundaries were across the area, and there was good moisture present. These conditions resulted in a thunderstorm that produced a landspout across the northeast Permian Basin and flash flooding in Scurry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Howard County and produced a landspout four miles northwest of Big Spring. It was located over an open field behind the old Holland Cotton Seed holding facility. No damage was reported by the observer and by the Big Spring EMC who went to the location. The path length and width were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable air mass supported several strong and severe thunderstorms this evening spanning portions of the far southwest Texas Panhandle.  Many of these storms were very slow moving and deposited significant rainfall estimated as great as five inches in some areas of eastern Parmer and western Castro Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by a Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) and severe (D2) drought conditions continued across Franklin, Titus, Camp, Morris, Marion, and Harrison Counties in Northeast Texas throughout the entire month of August, with continued below normal rainfall which ranged from three-quarters of an inch to around two inches across these areas. These monthly amounts were near to up to two inches below normal, with temperatures climbing into the upper 90s to near 100 degrees throughout much of the month, only exasperating drought conditions across these areas. By the second week of the month, extreme drought had spread into much of Franklin, Morris, Eastern Camp, and Western Marion Counties, which lingered into September.

Drought conditions eased for the first time in several months for portions of the South Plains and extreme southern Texas Panhandle. Parmer, Castro, Hall, and Childress Counties in the extreme southern Texas Panhandle were reduced to D1 (Moderate Drought) or lower while Bailey, Cochran, and Yoakum Counties in the western South Plains were as well. Elsewhere across the South Plains and Rolling Plains, D2 (Severe Drought) or higher remained in place. Although, drought conditions were generally reduced across all counties.||Part of this was due to widespread rainfall which came mostly from August 15th through August 20th. Several night time rounds of mesocale convective systems rolled out of eastern New Mexico bringing beneficial rainfall to much of the region but especially the extreme southern Texas Panhandle. More rainfall was needed to alleviate long term drought conditions across much of the South Plains and Rolling Plains. Generally, agricultural conditions improved because of the rainfall in advance of harvesting in the early fall.

Extreme (D3) and severe (D2) drought conditions continued through the first two weeks of August across Red River and Bowie County Texas, as very little rainfall fell through the first week of the month. A weak cool front shifted southeast to near the I-30 corridor of Northeast Texas and Southwest Arkansas by August 8th before becoming stationary, and helped focus scattered showers and thunderstorms over much of these areas, including Red River and Bowie Counties, through mid-month. Widespread rainfall amounts of one to three inches fell during this period, resulting in significant improvement to moderate drought (D1) by the middle of the month, which persisted into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was over the western part of the country with an upper trough over the Upper Midwest. Decent moisture and instability, along with modest wind shear, were across West Texas. An outflow boundary was approaching the area from storms that were across the Texas panhandle and Oklahoma. These conditions resulted in a thunderstorm with strong wind gusts across the northern Permian Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Dawson County and produced a 62 mph wind gust near Lamesa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a cold front moving toward the Permian Basin, and a mid-level disturbance was over the region. Storms developed south of Lubbock and slowly moved south towards the area. These slow moving storms, along with moisture in the lower atmosphere, resulted in flash flooding in parts of the Permian Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Gaines County and produced flash flooding in Seagraves. Cars were flooded out at the intersection of US 385 and TX 83. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough was over the southern and central Rocky Mountains and had flattened the upper ridge which had been over West Texas. There was a surface trough present with good instability across the area. Hot temperatures were across the area, along with fairly dry air below the cloud base and good moisture through the atmosphere. These conditions resulted in hail, thunderstorm wind damage, and flash flooding across portions of the Permian Basin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions were in place across most of McLennan County for all of August 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low developed over North Texas during August 11th-12th, which helped to enhance the seabreeze as it spread north northeast into East Texas and Western Louisiana during the afternoon hours on the 12th. These storms intensified during the afternoon as they surged northeast, producing a swath of damaging winds which downed numerous trees across East Texas. These storms weakened as they moved into extreme Northwest Louisiana during the late afternoon and early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Highway 94 near the Hudson community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough was over the southern and central Rocky Mountains and had flattened the upper ridge which had been over West Texas. There was a surface trough present with good instability across the area. Hot temperatures were across the area, along with fairly dry air below the cloud base and good moisture through the atmosphere. These conditions resulted in hail, thunderstorm wind damage, and flash flooding across portions of the Permian Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Ector County and produced flash flooding in Odessa. Motorists were stranded in Odessa. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough was moving over the Rockies with a surface trough near the Rio Grande helped to trigger thunderstorms over Hudspeth County.  An apparent microburst occurred in Dell City with one of the strongest storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Apparent microburst at the Dell City West Texas Mesonet Site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed by midday through the afternoon across portions of East Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and North Louisiana, near a weak surface front that extended across these areas in wake of an upper level low/trough passage over Arkansas. As these storms became more numerous during the afternoon once they became focused on multiple outflow boundary interactions, one particular storm briefly became severe over Eastern Cass County Texas, where a microburst produced damaging winds which broke several large tree limbs, tore an awning down, and resulted in roof damage to a building in Downtown Atlanta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were downed, an awning was blown off, and portions of a roof was peeled back of a building near the intersection of Main Street and North William Street in Downtown Atlanta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of evening and overnight thunderstorms moved out of the central Texas Panhandle into the extreme southern Texas Panhandle. One of these clusters became severe over Castro County producing a wind gust to 62 mph near Dimmitt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet site near Dimmitt measured a wind gust to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms early this evening targeted portions of Lamb County with damaging downbursts and heavy rain at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees, fences and about 20 utility poles were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions developed across Wood County Texas during the second week of August, as a prolonged period of dryness coupled with temperatures in the mid 90s to near 100 degrees throughout much of the month, resulted in deteriorating conditions. August monthly rainfall amounts ranged from one to two inches, with isolated higher amounts over the western sections of the county. These severe drought conditions continued into September across Wood County.

Severe (D2) drought conditions developed by the second week of August across Smith County Texas, as a prolonged period of dry conditions coupled with temperatures in the upper 90s to near 100 degrees carried over from late July. However, a weak cool front  which became stationary over Northeast Texas by August 10th focused periods of scattered showers and thunderstorms over Smith County, with rainfall amounts of one to in excess of two inches observed. Thus, drought conditions improved back to moderate (D1) by mid-month,  which persisted until late month before deteriorating to severe drought once again.
An isolated thunderstorm along the shore of Sabine Lake produced a landspout that touched down in Port Arthur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple videos taken around Port Arthur and Groves indicated a landspout briefly occurred near Sabine Lake and Pleasure Island. No damage was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Park Road Wildfire burned 557 acres in Burnet County. The fire started on July 29th and was contained on August 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Park Road Wildfire burned 557 acres in Burnet County. The fire started on July 29th and was contained on August 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over West Texas and stationary front combined to generate thunderstorms some of which produced heavy rain leading to flash flooding. An observer in Reagan Wells in Uvalde County reported 6.50 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding near FM 1051 and US Hwy 83 along the Nueces River near Concan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued moist and unstable air mass led to several thunderstorms this afternoon across the region. One of these storms produced a brief severe wind gust near Abernathy (Hale County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large part of South Central Texas received 50% or less of normal rain in August. This led to worsening drought conditions in Maverick (Exceptional D4), Zavala (D4), Caldwell (Severe D2), Comal (D2), Guadalupe (D2), Hays (D2), Kendall (D2), Real (D2), and Williamson (D2) Counties. Gillespie, Kinney, Llano, and Uvalde Counties remained in Extreme (D3) drought and Blanco, Burnet, Edwards, Kerr, and Val Verde were still in D2. Dimmit (D2) County saw some improvement. Fire danger at the end of the month was moderate.  All of the counties in D2 or worse drought had outdoor burn bans in effect. The seven day stream flow averages for the Pecos, Frio, Upper Guadalupe, and the Upper Colorado Rivers were much below normal (< 10%). The Rio Grande, Devils, and Nueces basins were below normal (10-24%). Llano was in Stage 5 water restrictions, Fredericksburg was in Stage 3, and Uvalde and Kerrville were in Stage 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large part of South Central Texas received 50% or less of normal rain in August. This led to worsening drought conditions in Maverick (Exceptional D4), Zavala (D4), Caldwell (Severe D2), Comal (D2), Guadalupe (D2), Hays (D2), Kendall (D2), Real (D2), and Williamson (D2) Counties. Gillespie, Kinney, Llano, and Uvalde Counties remained in Extreme (D3) drought and Blanco, Burnet, Edwards, Kerr, and Val Verde were still in D2. Dimmit (D2) County saw some improvement. Fire danger at the end of the month was moderate.  All of the counties in D2 or worse drought had outdoor burn bans in effect. The seven day stream flow averages for the Pecos, Frio, Upper Guadalupe, and the Upper Colorado Rivers were much below normal (< 10%). The Rio Grande, Devils, and Nueces basins were below normal (10-24%). Llano was in Stage 5 water restrictions, Fredericksburg was in Stage 3, and Uvalde and Kerrville were in Stage 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Graham Ranch Wildfire burned 392 acres six miles south of Kenedy in Karnes County. The fire started on the 20th and was contained on the 21st.

A stationary front provided focus for thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours Thursday through Saturday. Most of these storms remained below severe limits, but a few storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a tree down on CR 4680 south of the city of Boyd, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly developing line of thunderstorms raced northward through Cameron County east of US 77, creating gusts over 40 mph and causing minor damage to a few tree limbs, unfastened lawn furniture, and a few power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds of just over 45 mph knocked down several palm fronds and blew unfastened lawn furniture around portions of the City of Brownsville to Los Fresnos.  Brownsville/South Padre Island International Airport recorded a wind gust of 46 mph at 2:17 PM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly developing line of thunderstorms raced northward through Cameron County east of US 77, creating gusts over 40 mph and causing minor damage to a few tree limbs, unfastened lawn furniture, and a few power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds of between 45 and 52 mph knocked down several palm fronds and blew unfastened lawn furniture around portions of the City of Los Fresnos to Harlingen.  Harlingen/Valley International Airport recorded a wind gust of 52 mph at 2:46 PM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and little to no rainfall continued to maintain high evaporation rates as the pattern of La Canicula, an upper level ridge that suppresses precipitation while keeping or building hot temperatures, dominated the first half of August, followed by a broader ridge that covered much of the Gulf of Mexico and stretched back to Texas for the second half of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought maintained its grip on northern Zapata County through the 14th, and expanded slightly into central Zapata County through the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued hot and dry weather, along with moderate to high late summer evaporation rates, worsened drought conditions across the Rio Grande Plains of Zapata and Jim Hogg County to close out August 2018.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued dry and hotter than average weather through the end of August worsened drought across Zapata County.  Extreme (D3) conditions developed in northern portions of the county on the 21st, and spread into north central locations by the 28th.  Severe drought conditions spread into central Zapata County but no farther by month's end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions eased for the first time in several months for portions of the South Plains and extreme southern Texas Panhandle. Parmer, Castro, Hall, and Childress Counties in the extreme southern Texas Panhandle were reduced to D1 (Moderate Drought) or lower while Bailey, Cochran, and Yoakum Counties in the western South Plains were as well. Elsewhere across the South Plains and Rolling Plains, D2 (Severe Drought) or higher remained in place. Although, drought conditions were generally reduced across all counties.||Part of this was due to widespread rainfall which came mostly from August 15th through August 20th. Several night time rounds of mesocale convective systems rolled out of eastern New Mexico bringing beneficial rainfall to much of the region but especially the extreme southern Texas Panhandle. More rainfall was needed to alleviate long term drought conditions across much of the South Plains and Rolling Plains. Generally, agricultural conditions improved because of the rainfall in advance of harvesting in the early fall.

Although, drought conditions were generally reduced across all counties.||Part of this was due to widespread rainfall which came mostly from August 15th through August 20th. Several night time rounds of mesocale convective systems rolled out of eastern New Mexico bringing beneficial rainfall to much of the region but especially the extreme southern Texas Panhandle. More rainfall was needed to alleviate long term drought conditions across much of the South Plains and Rolling Plains. Generally, agricultural conditions improved because of the rainfall in advance of harvesting in the early fall.

A mesoscale convective system (MCS) developed over the Texas Panhandle and moved east during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 22nd. However, a very hot and deeply mixed atmosphere had developed from east-central New Mexico into the extreme southwestern Texas Panhandle creating an unstable atmosphere. Isolated thunderstorms developed initially in eastern New Mexico before moving into the extreme southwestern Panhandle. A couple of these storms became severe while in Parmer County producing severe winds and quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report from the public indicated hail to the size of quarters eight miles west of Lazbuddie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of the 24th, a mesoscale convective system tracked eastward across the Texas Panhandle. This complex of thunderstorms generated a strong outflow boundary that created severe wind gusts in the south-central and southeastern Texas Panhandle. Many of these wind gusts associated with the outflow boundary were well ahead of the thunderstorm complex. Scattered thunderstorms had developed along the outflow boundary in Hall County near Memphis which developed a severe thunderstorm wind gust.||Wind gust reports are from the Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet and Childress ASOS:||79 mph at Dimmitt (Castro County), 67 mph at Happy (Swisher County), 66 mph at Lesley (Hall County), 66 mph at Childress (Childress County), 58 mph at Estelline (Hall County), and 58 mph at Turkey (Hall County).
A cold front moved into the region. This resulted in scattered storms along with an isolated strong to severe storm in Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported numerous trees and powerlines down east of Vidor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave moved into South Texas on June 18th. A series of upper level disturbances interacted with this wave throughout its life cycle. This helped to maintain a broad area of low pressure at all levels of the atmosphere for the period from June 18th through June 20th across South Texas and the adjacent Gulf waters. Several boundaries and convergence along the coastline aided the production of heavy rainfall. ||Most of the rain occurred between the early morning hours of June 19th through the morning of the 20th. Some of these storms produced rainfall rates as high as 5-6 inches per hour. Storm total rainfall amounts from Monday (June 18) morning through Thursday (June 21) morning were as high as 15 to 18 inches across southern and southeastern Jim Wells County, extreme northwest Kleberg County, isolated locations in Nueces County, central San Patricio County, Aransas County, and western Refugio County. ||Even though most of South Texas was in a moderate to extreme drought before the heavy rains fell, flooding still occurred due to the intense rainfall rates which produced copious amounts of rainfall. Flooding of many streets, some with several feet of water, were quite common across many South Texas communities, generally east of Highway 281. Some of the hardest affected cities and towns were Alfred, Alice, Aransas Pass, Bonnie View, Corpus Christi, La Gloria, Odem, Orange Grove, Port Aransas, Premont, Rios, Rockport, Sinton, and Woodsboro. Several water rescues, especially in Corpus Christi, had to be performed due to the depth of the water stalling out vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water levels on Lattas Creek caused flooding of Farm to Market Road 665 near Alice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of the Hill Country and Rio Grande Plains region received less than 25% of normal precipitation during the month of June. Drought conditions worsened across South Central Texas with Kinney and Maverick Counties entering exceptional drought category (Stage D4), Dimmit, Uvalde, Real, Val Verde, and Zavala moving into extreme category (D3), and Bandera, Comal, Edwards, Frio, Gillespie, Kendall, Kerr, and Llano in severe category (D2). Of these counties Dimmit, Edwards, Frio, Gillespie, Kerr, Kinney, Llano, Maverick, Val Verde, and Zavala had outdoor burn bans in effect at the end of the month. The main effects of the drought conditions were hydrological. The seven day stream flow averages for the Rio Grande, Pecos, Nueces, Guadalupe, and Colorado Rivers were below normal (10-24%) at the end of the month. Lake Amistad was 32.9 feet below Conservation Pool level, Medina Lake was 24.9 feet below, Canyon Lake was 5.1 feet below, and Lake Buchanan was 6.2 below. The Edwards Aquifer was 5.1 feet below average and 3.0 feet below the 2017 level. Fredericksburg was in Stage 3 water restrictions, New Braunfels was in Stage 2, and Uvalde was in Stage 1.

Lake Amistad was 32.9 feet below Conservation Pool level, Medina Lake was 24.9 feet below, Canyon Lake was 5.1 feet below, and Lake Buchanan was 6.2 below. The Edwards Aquifer was 5.1 feet below average and 3.0 feet below the 2017 level. Fredericksburg was in Stage 3 water restrictions, New Braunfels was in Stage 2, and Uvalde was in Stage 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave moved into South Texas on June 18th. A series of upper level disturbances interacted with this wave throughout its life cycle. This helped to maintain a broad area of low pressure at all levels of the atmosphere for the period from June 18th through June 20th across South Texas and the adjacent Gulf waters. Several boundaries and convergence along the coastline aided the production of heavy rainfall. ||Most of the rain occurred between the early morning hours of June 19th through the morning of the 20th. Some of these storms produced rainfall rates as high as 5-6 inches per hour. Storm total rainfall amounts from Monday (June 18) morning through Thursday (June 21) morning were as high as 15 to 18 inches across southern and southeastern Jim Wells County, extreme northwest Kleberg County, isolated locations in Nueces County, central San Patricio County, Aransas County, and western Refugio County. ||Even though most of South Texas was in a moderate to extreme drought before the heavy rains fell, flooding still occurred due to the intense rainfall rates which produced copious amounts of rainfall. Flooding of many streets, some with several feet of water, were quite common across many South Texas communities, generally east of Highway 281. Some of the hardest affected cities and towns were Alfred, Alice, Aransas Pass, Bonnie View, Corpus Christi, La Gloria, Odem, Orange Grove, Port Aransas, Premont, Rios, Rockport, Sinton, and Woodsboro. Several water rescues, especially in Corpus Christi, had to be performed due to the depth of the water stalling out vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: TXDOT closed Farm to Market Road 1538 northwest of La Gloria due to water flooding the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of tropical storm Bud continue to pump moisture up to the Panhandles. Storms developed over NM and moved into the TX Panhandle.  One storm managed to have a 70 mph severe wind gust in Oldham County.

Thunderstorms formed in a warm, moist airmass in the vicinity of stationary front over Southeast Texas and moved to the west. Some of these storms produced damaging wind gusts, large hail, and heavy rain over the northeastern part of South Central Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding closing roads in and around Round Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed in a warm, moist airmass in the vicinity of stationary front over Southeast Texas and moved to the west. Some of these storms produced damaging wind gusts, large hail, and heavy rain over the northeastern part of South Central Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding washing away a vehicle near Cypress Creek. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level ridge retreated to the north and allowed thunderstorms to develop in the southeasterly flow off the Gulf of Mexico. Some of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.
The upper level ridge retreated to the north and allowed thunderstorms to develop in the southeasterly flow off the Gulf of Mexico. Some of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced wind gusts estimated at 75 mph that uprooted a tree, knocked down some wood fencing and some tree limbs, and collapsed a metal awning in northwestern San Antonio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level ridge retreated to the north and allowed thunderstorms to develop in the southeasterly flow off the Gulf of Mexico. Some of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced wind gusts estimated at 65 mph that knocked down some large tree limbs in the Wildwood subdivision in northwestern San Antonio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and dry conditions continued into June as an upper level ridge of high pressure persisted across South Texas for the first half of the month. Severe drought over portions of the western Brush Country and over the Coastal Bend worsened by the middle of the month to extreme and expanded in areal coverage. Extreme drought developed over the northern Coastal Bend. However, a slow moving upper level low along with abnormally high moisture provided a period of heavy rains across the Coastal Bend on the 19th and 20th. This alleviated the drought conditions over the coastal plains. Severe drought persisted through the month over the northwest portions of the Brush Country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought occurred over most of the county through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave moved into South Texas on June 18th. A series of upper level disturbances interacted with this wave throughout its life cycle. This helped to maintain a broad area of low pressure at all levels of the atmosphere for the period from June 18th through June 20th across South Texas and the adjacent Gulf waters. Several boundaries and convergence along the coastline aided the production of heavy rainfall. ||Most of the rain occurred between the early morning hours of June 19th through the morning of the 20th. Some of these storms produced rainfall rates as high as 5-6 inches per hour. Storm total rainfall amounts from Monday (June 18) morning through Thursday (June 21) morning were as high as 15 to 18 inches across southern and southeastern Jim Wells County, extreme northwest Kleberg County, isolated locations in Nueces County, central San Patricio County, Aransas County, and western Refugio County. ||Even though most of South Texas was in a moderate to extreme drought before the heavy rains fell, flooding still occurred due to the intense rainfall rates which produced copious amounts of rainfall. Flooding of many streets, some with several feet of water, were quite common across many South Texas communities, generally east of Highway 281. Some of the hardest affected cities and towns were Alfred, Alice, Aransas Pass, Bonnie View, Corpus Christi, La Gloria, Odem, Orange Grove, Port Aransas, Premont, Rios, Rockport, Sinton, and Woodsboro. Several water rescues, especially in Corpus Christi, had to be performed due to the depth of the water stalling out vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain to the northwest of Beeville in the watershed of Poesta Creek led to flooding in Beeville. Low lying areas along Poesta Creek were flooded with road closure barricades set up. Farm to Market Roads 673 and 351 were flooded. A few water rescues were conducted for people driving around barricades.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and dry conditions continued into June as an upper level ridge of high pressure persisted across South Texas for the first half of the month. Severe drought over portions of the western Brush Country and over the Coastal Bend worsened by the middle of the month to extreme and expanded in areal coverage. Extreme drought developed over the northern Coastal Bend. However, a slow moving upper level low along with abnormally high moisture provided a period of heavy rains across the Coastal Bend on the 19th and 20th. This alleviated the drought conditions over the coastal plains. Severe drought persisted through the month over the northwest portions of the Brush Country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought over the county worsened to extreme drought for the southern part of the county by the 12th. Heavy rains on the 19th and 20th relieved the drought over the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front pushed through much of the Ark-La-Tex on June 3rd, and became stationary from the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles southeast across Northcentral and Deep East Texas and Central Louisiana. Meanwhile, a shortwave trough shifted southeast across much of Oklahoma into North Texas during the afternoon and evening hours of June 4th, along the stationary front, thus igniting scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions of East Texas during peak heating. Given the forcing aloft near the front within a hot, humid, and unstable air mass, some of these storms became severe across Northern Smith and Nacogdoches Counties, with damaging winds downing trees across these areas, as well as marginally severe hail falling north of Tyler. In addition, a 61 year old female was injured when lightning struck a nearby tree for a second time from these storms northeast of Tyler.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 53 knots (61 mph) was measured at the Al Mangham Jr. Regional Airport just southwest of Nacogdoches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of a mesoscale convective system (MCS) that developed during the early morning hours on June 7th over Eastern Oklahoma, migrated into Southeast Oklahoma, portions of Southwest Arkansas, and into extreme Northeast Texas during the afternoon hours, along a weak shortwave trough aloft that drifted south over these areas along the eastern periphery of an upper level ridge over the Southern Plains. Mesoscale boundaries from these earlier storms allowed for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon during peak heating, within a hot and unstable air mass in place. Some of these storms briefly became severe over Central Red River County Texas, which produced damaging winds which downed large limbs southwest of Clarksville. These storms also moved very little and produced locally heavy rainfall in and near Clarksville, which resulted in localized flash flooding which flooded a vehicle in town. These storms quickly diminished during the evening with the loss of heating and instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down on FM 909 near Scatter Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Southern Plains upper level ridge of high pressure began to break down during the daytime hours on June 10th, allowing for a weak upper level trough to drift south into Northeast Texas, extreme Southern Arkansas, and Northern Louisiana. A deepening south-southwest low to mid level flow resulted in an increase in Gulf moisture north across these areas, which when combined with afternoon temperatures in the lower to mid 90s, resulted in moderate instability and the development of isolated to widely scattered showers and thunderstorms. One of these storms became severe over Southeastern Smith County Texas, resulting in numerous trees and power lines to be blown down in the community of Arp. These storms quickly diminished during the evening with the loss of heating and instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down in Arp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much above normal temperatures and much below normal rainfall contributed to severe drought conditions developing across portions of Northeast Texas including much of Wood, Franklin, Camp, Upshur, and Harrison Counties. Only 0.95 inches of rain fell from early May through all of June in Marshall (Harrison County), which was 9% of normal during what is typically the wettest period of the year. Only 1.43 inches of rain also fell from early May throughout all of June in Mount Vernon (Franklin County), which was 16% of normal. This extensive dryness, combined with temperatures that ranged from 3-6 degrees above normal during May, and 3-5 degrees above normal during June, resulted in many farmers/producers losing many of their non-irrigated crops, as well as increased instances of wildfires and heightened fire danger.

On the evening of the 24th, a mesoscale convective system tracked eastward across the Texas Panhandle. This complex of thunderstorms generated a strong outflow boundary that created severe wind gusts in the south-central and southeastern Texas Panhandle. Many of these wind gusts associated with the outflow boundary were well ahead of the thunderstorm complex. Scattered thunderstorms had developed along the outflow boundary in Hall County near Memphis which developed a severe thunderstorm wind gust.||Wind gust reports are from the Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet and Childress ASOS:||79 mph at Dimmitt (Castro County), 67 mph at Happy (Swisher County), 66 mph at Lesley (Hall County), 66 mph at Childress (Childress County), 58 mph at Estelline (Hall County), and 58 mph at Turkey (Hall County).

An upper ridge was over the region with moisture across the area.  A weak upper level disturbance combined with strong heating and low and mid-level moisture to produce thunderstorms across the northern Permian Basin and Western Low Rolling Plains. These storms produced strong, damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Scurry County and produced wind damage in the southeast part of the county. Severe winds overturned a manufactured home near CR 4136 and CR 465 that resulted in one injury. This home was properly anchored to the ground but the survey team found that the estimated 110-120 mph winds caused the tie-down straps to fail and the house to roll from its original location. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was over the region with moisture across the area.  A weak upper level disturbance combined with strong heating and low and mid-level moisture to produce thunderstorms across the northern Permian Basin and Western Low Rolling Plains. These storms produced strong, damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Scurry County and produced wind damage across the southeast portion of Scurry County. Winds were around 100 to 110 mph and snapped and mangled over 200 wooden power poles. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The FM99 wildfire quickly burned 541 acres in Karnes County on June 12th. The day was hot, dry, and breezy. High temperatures in the area were between 94 and 99 degrees with minimum relative humidity between 25% and 37%. Winds were from the southeast at 14 to 17 mph with gusts up to 24 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The FM99 wildfire quickly burned 541 acres in Karnes County on June 12th. The day was hot, dry, and breezy. High temperatures in the area were between 94 and 99 degrees with minimum relative humidity between 25% and 37%. Winds were from the southeast at 14 to 17 mph with gusts up to 24 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported quarter-sized hail at the Courthouse in the city of McKinney, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous slow moving thunderstorms moved from the South Plains into the Rolling Plains this Sunday afternoon. In addition to heavy rain and minor flooding, one of these storms produced a severe wind gust near White River Lake in Crosby County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by a Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms with a large swath of high winds moved northeast from northern Dawson County into far southwest Lynn County before decaying a short while later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three power poles were downed along Farm to Market Road 213.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave moved into South Texas on June 18th. A series of upper level disturbances interacted with this wave throughout its life cycle. This helped to maintain a broad area of low pressure at all levels of the atmosphere for the period from June 18th through June 20th across South Texas and the adjacent Gulf waters. Several boundaries and convergence along the coastline aided the production of heavy rainfall. ||Most of the rain occurred between the early morning hours of June 19th through the morning of the 20th. Some of these storms produced rainfall rates as high as 5-6 inches per hour. Storm total rainfall amounts from Monday (June 18) morning through Thursday (June 21) morning were as high as 15 to 18 inches across southern and southeastern Jim Wells County, extreme northwest Kleberg County, isolated locations in Nueces County, central San Patricio County, Aransas County, and western Refugio County. ||Even though most of South Texas was in a moderate to extreme drought before the heavy rains fell, flooding still occurred due to the intense rainfall rates which produced copious amounts of rainfall. Flooding of many streets, some with several feet of water, were quite common across many South Texas communities, generally east of Highway 281. Some of the hardest affected cities and towns were Alfred, Alice, Aransas Pass, Bonnie View, Corpus Christi, La Gloria, Odem, Orange Grove, Port Aransas, Premont, Rios, Rockport, Sinton, and Woodsboro. Several water rescues, especially in Corpus Christi, had to be performed due to the depth of the water stalling out vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flooded the road on Farm to Market Road 1329 near Rios.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface boundary coupled with disturbances in northwest flow aloft generated  multiple rounds of thunderstorms. The strongest storms occurred late Tuesday night into early Wednesday morning, when large and damaging hail fell over parts of the Dallas/Ft Worth Metroplex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio reported quarter-sized hail in the city of Plano, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was over the region with an upper trough moving east over the western part of the country. A surface trough was present from the Texas panhandle south to the Big Bend region. A dryline extended from the Permian Basin south to near Fort Stockton. Very good surface heating, moisture, and instability were across the area. These conditions allowed for thunderstorms with large hail and strong winds to develop across the northern Permian Basin and Western Low Rolling Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Borden County and produced a 69 mph wind gust near Gail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of June 13th, as a weak sea breeze moved inland from the extreme Southeast Texas and Southwest Louisiana coasts. A moist air mass in place and afternoon temperatures in the mid 90s contributed to moderate instability over Deep East Texas, allowing for these isolated showers and thunderstorms to continue developing through the late afternoon hours. One of these storms briefly become strong, where a localized microburst contributed to sub-severe wind gusts of 45-55 mph over Northwest Nacogdoches County southwest of Cushing between the Lilbert and Looneyville communities, which uprooted a weakened but top-heavy tree, snapped several branches, and peeled off a portion of a metal roof off of an outbuilding. These storms dissipated around sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A localized but non-severe microburst with estimated wind gusts between 45-55 mph uprooted a weakened but top-heavy 200 year old sweet gum tree in front of a home on County Road 343 between the Lilbert and Looneyville communities. This tree landed near a parked car and damaged the windshield of the vehicle. Several branches were snapped, and a metal roof was peeled off of an outbuilding. Small hail was observed as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS moving southeastward out of Kansas on the morning of the 7th brought significant flooding to central OK as well as several severe wind and hail reports. Later in the evening a second set of storms formed near the dryline in the panhandles and brought severe winds to western north Texas. A subsequent wake low produced gusty winds over southwest Oklahoma as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across some of the Central Texas counties during the second half of June 2018.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across Limestone County during the third week of June and continued through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions popped up across more of North and Central Texas towards the end of June as below-normal rainfall continued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D3) drought conditions developed across Hood County during the final week of June, 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was an upper trough over the central part of the country which resulted in the upper ridge moving just south of the region. A cold front was across the central/southern Permian Basin and it was slowly moving south. These conditions resulted in a storm developing across the Lower Trans Pecos with large hail.

An upper ridge was over the region with moisture across the area.  A weak upper level disturbance combined with strong heating and low and mid-level moisture to produce thunderstorms across the northern Permian Basin and Western Low Rolling Plains. These storms produced strong, damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Scurry County and produced a 76 mph wind gust near Snyder.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was over the region with moisture across the area.  A weak upper level disturbance combined with strong heating and low and mid-level moisture to produce thunderstorms across the northern Permian Basin and Western Low Rolling Plains. These storms produced strong, damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Scurry County and produced a 64 mph wind gust near Snyder.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture streaming overhead from remnants of eastern Pacific tropical system Bud fueled isolated to scattered thunderstorms from eastern New Mexico into the South Plains on the afternoon of the 16th. Very warm near surface temperatures allowed a few of these thunderstorms to produce severe wind gusts. One such storm produced damage in the city of Levelland (Hockley County) which downed power lines and ripped metal roofs off two structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet site near Levelland measured a wind gust to 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought levels remained at D2 (Severe) drought conditions or greater for most of the region. However, there was some spotty improvement over the Rolling Plains. Portions of Kent, Stonewall, Dickens, and Crosby Counties dropped into D0 (Abnormally Dry) to D1 (Moderate) drought conditions. June was typically hotter and drier compared to normal for most areas on the Caprock with the fifth hottest June on record for Lubbock. Childress recorded its 14th hottest June. This was mostly due to a typical summertime pattern developing in early June when it typically does not occur until late June. Upper level high pressure was located just to the south of the region for most of the month. Occasional upper level disturbances to the north of the region brought scattered shower activity on the Caprock and more seasonal rain across the Rolling Plains.

June was typically hotter and drier compared to normal for most areas on the Caprock with the fifth hottest June on record for Lubbock. Childress recorded its 14th hottest June. This was mostly due to a typical summertime pattern developing in early June when it typically does not occur until late June. Upper level high pressure was located just to the south of the region for most of the month. Occasional upper level disturbances to the north of the region brought scattered shower activity on the Caprock and more seasonal rain across the Rolling Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was over the region with moisture across the area.  A weak upper level disturbance combined with strong heating and low and mid-level moisture to produce thunderstorms across the northern Permian Basin and Western Low Rolling Plains. These storms produced strong, damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Scurry County and produced wind damage near Snyder. An 18-wheeler was blown over south of Snyder on Highway 208. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Scurry County and produced wind damage across the southeast portion of Scurry County. Winds were around 100 to 110 mph and snapped and mangled over 200 wooden power poles. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture streaming overhead from remnants of eastern Pacific tropical system Bud fueled isolated to scattered thunderstorms from eastern New Mexico into the South Plains on the afternoon of the 16th. Very warm near surface temperatures allowed a few of these thunderstorms to produce severe wind gusts. One such storm produced damage in the city of Levelland (Hockley County) which downed power lines and ripped metal roofs off two structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet site near Earth measured a wind gust to 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture streaming overhead from remnants of eastern Pacific tropical system Bud fueled isolated to scattered thunderstorms from eastern New Mexico into the South Plains on the afternoon of the 16th. Very warm near surface temperatures allowed a few of these thunderstorms to produce severe wind gusts. One such storm produced damage in the city of Levelland (Hockley County) which downed power lines and ripped metal roofs off two structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1705 CST, a Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet site four miles south of Levelland measured a wind gust to 58 mph. About 14 minutes later, the Hockley County Sheriff's department reported several instances of wind damage in town, including downed power lines and two metal roofs ripped off structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was over the region with moisture across the area.  A weak upper level disturbance combined with strong heating and low and mid-level moisture to produce thunderstorms across the northern Permian Basin and Western Low Rolling Plains. These storms produced strong, damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Scurry County and produced wind damage in the southeast part of the county. Straight-line winds near 85 to 90 mph ripped apart a metal barn near FM 1606 and CR 4105. Winds blew into the southeast facing opening of the building, lifting the roof and blowing out the walls. Parts of the metal roof were blown about 300 yards downwind, or northwest, of the original location of the barn. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was over the region with moisture across the area. These storms produced strong, damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Mitchell County and produced wind damage in the northeast part of the county. A small barn with iron girders had been found totally destroyed along CR 452, near CR 458. Pieces of the barn had been blown into the adjacent field to the west southwest. This damage suggests straight-line winds nearing 115 mph occurred. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Mitchell County and produced wind damage in the northeast part of the county. A total of 86 steel lattice transmission towers were found either bent or completely broken in a line from northeast Mitchell County southwest to the south side of Interstate 20. This damage indicates wind speeds of 110-115 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was over the region with moisture across the area.  A weak upper level disturbance combined with strong heating and low and mid-level moisture to produce thunderstorms across the northern Permian Basin and Western Low Rolling Plains. These storms produced strong, damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms fell across northeast Mitchell County and produced flash flooding. A pickup truck was found washed off the I-20 feeder road at North Fork Champion Creek. The water line on the bridge suggested the water was near 10 feet deep over the feeder road at one point. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture associated with the remnants of eastern Pacific tropical system Bud moved over top of the region of the 18th. Hot surface temperatures in the Rolling Plains allowed instability to build to create isolated to widely scattered thunderstorms. One of these thunderstorms became severe as it moved near Graham producing a wind gust to 59 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet site near Graham measured a wind gust to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS moving southeastward out of Kansas on the morning of the 7th brought significant flooding to central OK as well as several severe wind and hail reports. Later in the evening a second set of storms formed near the dryline in the panhandles and brought severe winds to western north Texas. A subsequent wake low produced gusty winds over southwest Oklahoma as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Passing outflow boundary produced strong winds that downed power lines in Archer City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred through Guadalupe Pass behind a cold front.

Very hot temperatures characterized by highs of 102 to 107 degrees coupled with a dryline focused high based thunderstorm development near the Lubbock area this afternoon. As these storms moved east into richer moisture in Crosby County, one storm escalated significantly in intensity.  This supercell thunderstorm delivered major wind and hail damage to Ralls and continued to move east through Crosbyton where several utility poles were downed.  The storm then turned more southeast and struck Spur with very large hail before producing a small, brief tornado over open land south of Jayton.  The greatest destruction occurred on the west side of Ralls where three large cotton compress buildings were completely destroyed by straight line winds determined to be as high as 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by a Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front pushed through much of the Ark-La-Tex on June 3rd, and became stationary from the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles southeast across Northcentral and Deep East Texas and Central Louisiana. Meanwhile, a shortwave trough shifted southeast across much of Oklahoma into North Texas during the afternoon and evening hours of June 4th, along the stationary front, thus igniting scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions of East Texas during peak heating. Given the forcing aloft near the front within a hot, humid, and unstable air mass, some of these storms became severe across Northern Smith and Nacogdoches Counties, with damaging winds downing trees across these areas, as well as marginally severe hail falling north of Tyler. In addition, a 61 year old female was injured when lightning struck a nearby tree for a second time from these storms northeast of Tyler.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large hardwood trees were blown down near a home on County Road 26, all of which were lying in the same direction.

Meanwhile, a shortwave trough shifted southeast across much of Oklahoma into North Texas during the afternoon and evening hours of June 4th, along the stationary front, thus igniting scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions of East Texas during peak heating. Given the forcing aloft near the front within a hot, humid, and unstable air mass, some of these storms became severe across Northern Smith and Nacogdoches Counties, with damaging winds downing trees across these areas, as well as marginally severe hail falling north of Tyler. In addition, a 61 year old female was injured when lightning struck a nearby tree for a second time from these storms northeast of Tyler.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree at 10672 County Road 383 northeast of Tyler, with a tree limb falling on a car at the residence. A 61 year old female went outside to investigate the damage when lightning struck the nearby tree once again, throwing the female down to the ground. She was taken by ambulance to the hospital but other than serious tingling throughout her body, she was found to not have suffered significant injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave moved into South Texas on June 18th. A series of upper level disturbances interacted with this wave throughout its life cycle. This helped to maintain a broad area of low pressure at all levels of the atmosphere for the period from June 18th through June 20th across South Texas and the adjacent Gulf waters. Several boundaries and convergence along the coastline aided the production of heavy rainfall. ||Most of the rain occurred between the early morning hours of June 19th through the morning of the 20th. Some of these storms produced rainfall rates as high as 5-6 inches per hour. Storm total rainfall amounts from Monday (June 18) morning through Thursday (June 21) morning were as high as 15 to 18 inches across southern and southeastern Jim Wells County, extreme northwest Kleberg County, isolated locations in Nueces County, central San Patricio County, Aransas County, and western Refugio County. ||Even though most of South Texas was in a moderate to extreme drought before the heavy rains fell, flooding still occurred due to the intense rainfall rates which produced copious amounts of rainfall. Flooding of many streets, some with several feet of water, were quite common across many South Texas communities, generally east of Highway 281. Some of the hardest affected cities and towns were Alfred, Alice, Aransas Pass, Bonnie View, Corpus Christi, La Gloria, Odem, Orange Grove, Port Aransas, Premont, Rios, Rockport, Sinton, and Woodsboro. Several water rescues, especially in Corpus Christi, had to be performed due to the depth of the water stalling out vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS moving southeastward out of Kansas on the morning of the 7th brought significant flooding to central OK as well as several severe wind and hail reports. Later in the evening a second set of storms formed near the dryline in the panhandles and brought severe winds to western north Texas. A subsequent wake low produced gusty winds over southwest Oklahoma as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by Sheppard Air Force Base ASOS in Wichita Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper ridge over the region was flattened somewhat by an upper level disturbance that moved over the area. There were outflow boundaries that slowly moved into the area from earlier thunderstorms across the Texas panhandle and Oklahoma. A dryline was present across the eastern Permian Basin, Western Low Rolling Plains, and Lower Trans Pecos. Intense surface heating allowed for very good instability across the area. This combination of lift, moisture, and instability contributed to thunderstorms with large hail developing across the Western Low Rolling Plains and far eastern Permian Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the afternoon on the 29th, scattered thunderstorms developed across eastern New Mexico and moved into West Texas. Strong instability allowed several of these storms to become severe with strong wind gusts spanning from Lake Alan Henry (Garza County) and White River Lake (Crosby County) to Childress (Childress County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet site near Lake Alan Henry measured a wind gust to 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough moved into the region and interacted with an upper level low. The first day of heavy rain in Southeast Texas produced 4 to 8 inches and caused isolated flooding. A second round of rain produced several more inches and caused more flooding in Orange and Jefferson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Underpasses flooded along MLK Blvd with at least one car stalled during the heavy rain event where near 8 inches fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) tracked across the Texas Panhandle during the early morning hours of the 20th. This MCS produced a strong outflow boundary on the south end of the system which was able to create a severe wind gust to 61 mph at the Childress Municipal Airport ASOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong outflow winds became severe at the Childress Municipal Airport recording a wind gust to 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) developed over the Texas Panhandle and moved east during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 22nd. However, a very hot and deeply mixed atmosphere had developed from east-central New Mexico into the extreme southwestern Texas Panhandle creating an unstable atmosphere. Isolated thunderstorms developed initially in eastern New Mexico before moving into the extreme southwestern Panhandle. A couple of these storms became severe while in Parmer County producing severe winds and quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet site near Friona measured a wind gust to 65 mph.

An upper level trough was over the Rocky Mountains with a surface trough across southeast New Mexico. This surface trough with southeast surface winds contributed to abundant low-level moisture being transported across the area. A dryline was across West Texas with very unstable air along and east of this feature. The lift from the trough and dryline, good moisture and instability, and good wind shear resulted in thunderstorms developing with strong, damaging winds and large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Zapata County, which missed the lion's share of the torrential rainfall between June 18 and 22, 2018, that produced near record to record event rainfall and left an estimated more than $250 million in public and private property damage, saw its drought condition worsen from abnormally dry at the start of the month to severe by the last week of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Monthly rainfall of about one half inch in northwest Zapata County - or between 10 and 25 percent of normal - combined with similar rains in June and very little in prior months - allowed drought conditions to worsen through July, with Severe (D2) Drought arriving in the northwest corner of the county by the 24th, continuing through the end of July and eventually worsening further by late August.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Management Coordinator from Willacy County, Texas, reported a small tornado briefly touched down between Raymondville and San Perlita. There was no structural damage, but patches of sugar cane were blown down based on photo evidence, and a short video confirmed the brief touch down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening upper level trough over southern Nevaded throughout the morning hours set the stages for active weather downstream across the Panhandles. Vertically stacked speed shear aloft out ahead of the main upper level system; in-conjunction with good lift and a rather strong low level jet at 850 mb of around 40 kts set the stages for linear convection to develop across the northern Panhandles along and out ahead of a surface convergence boundary. With SBCAPE around 3000 J/lg and effective bulk shear around 40-50 kts, a good environment had storms congeal into a line and move northeast. Early on, storms across the region produce hail from quarters up to tennis ball size. A second round of storms developed across the southeast Texas Panhandle with additional severe reports including a funnel cloud reported in Wheeler County. Storms quickly exited the region as early evening on the 2nd approached.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail between penny and quarter sized reported, with accumulation in spots. Heavy rainfall also has occurred with recent storms.

A large upper level storm system over the desert southwest of the United States fostered the development of dryline convection over the far eastern Rolling Plains on the afternoon of the second. One of these storms became severe in Stonewall County before exiting into northwest Texas. Late on the second into the early morning hours of the third, a strong low level jet developed allowing thunderstorms to expand into the eastern South Plains. One of these storms produced a severe wind gust at Slaton (Lubbock County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet site near Aspermont measured a wind gust to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 15th, a cold front pushed into the southern South Plains initially developing thunderstorms in the Permian Basin. However, strong instability north of the frontal boundary combined with an advancing short wave in New Mexico brought significant severe convection to the South Plains and extreme southern Texas Panhandle. Initially, thunderstorms brought severe hail to the area. Crosbyton (Crosby County) took the brunt of the damage with widespread damage reports in the town due to wind-driven baseball size hail. An NWS storm survey the next day revealed numerous south facing windows on buildings destroyed along with many totaled vehicles. In Abernathy (Hale County), penny to quarter size hail completely covered Interstate-27. As the evening progressed, storms moving into the area from New Mexico primarily created severe wind gusts. A large swath of wind gusts spanned from western Hale County into central Floyd County. These strong wind gusts blew over a tractor trailer on Interstate-27 just north of Plainview (Hale County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet measured a wind gust to 67 mph near Ralls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed over the South Plains on the afternoon of the 18th. One of these storms became severe as it moved over northwestern portions of the city of Lubbock (Lubbock County) and dropped one inch size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters was reported near 4th Street and Milwaukee Avenue in Lubbock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several discrete supercells formed off a dryline in the eastern parts of the panhandles in the late afternoon of the 2nd. These storms moved eastward through the evening, eventually congealing into a line before coming across central Oklahoma. QLCS tornadoes became a significant hazard at this point. After this round of storms made its way out of the area, scattered storms began to form from central Texas up into south western Oklahoma overnight, moving generally northeast into the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of strong thunderstorm wind damage occurred northeast of Oklaunion. Tree and house damage was noted on county road 117 N just north of US-287, near US-287 and county road 128 E, and along FM-1763.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several discrete supercells formed off a dryline in the eastern parts of the panhandles in the late afternoon of the 2nd. These storms moved eastward through the evening, eventually congealing into a line before coming across central Oklahoma. QLCS tornadoes became a significant hazard at this point. After this round of storms made its way out of the area, scattered storms began to form from central Texas up into south western Oklahoma overnight, moving generally northeast into the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Windthorst High School gymnasium had significant roof damage from thunderstorm winds. A greenhouse was also destroyed at the school, and a nearby barbecue restaurant sustained roof damage on the southwest side of the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level trough developed over the Four Corners Region on December 26th, before taking on a negative tilt as it ejected northeast across Northwest and North Texas and much of Oklahoma during the early morning hours of the 27th. Strong southerly winds ahead of this upper trough resulted in warm, humid conditions spreading north across East Texas, North Louisiana, and extreme Southern Arkansas, resulting in weak to moderate surface based instability over these areas. Wind shear was also very strong ahead of this system, with the rapidly increasing moisture return and subtle forcing ahead of the trough allowing for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and especially during the evening over East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and adjacent sections of Southwest Arkansas. Isolated supercell thunderstorms developed over portions of East Texas during the evening, with several reports of large hail and damaging winds over Smith County. Meanwhile, a squall line developed just to the west over Northern and Central Texas along the ejecting upper trough, with these thunderstorms racing east across East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, and North Louisiana during the late evening through the overnight hours of the 26th-27th. Numerous reports of damaging winds which downed trees and power lines were reported from this squall line over East Texas, before the strongest storms quickly exited this area after 1 AM on the 27th.||In addition to the damaging winds and large hail, locally heavy rainfall also accompanied these storms, with rainfall amounts ranging from two to four inches with isolated higher amounts. Much of these rains fell over an extended period of time, and given the already saturated grounds from much above normal rainfall that fell since September, many smaller creeks, streams, and bayous began to overflow their banks which flooded/closed numerous roadways across East Texas well after the heavy rainfall had ended during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a gas well on County Road 350, causing a large fire. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level disturbance and a Pacific Cold front interacted with a moist and unstable airmass across the Big Country or the region along and north of Interstate 20.  Surface dewpoints were in the upper 60s.  A line of thunderstorms developed and resulted in several damaging thunderstorm wind reports.
Wind gusts to 60 mph occurred at Cotulla on the 13th behind a strong cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 60 mph from the northwest occurred at Cotulla-La Salle County airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid/upper level low quickly moved east across the Rolling|Plains of Texas in the afternoon hours of the 13th.  Very strong low level winds fields are the result of rapid pressure falls as the low continues to strengthen through |the evening hours. As a result, high wind criteria (sustained winds 40 MPH or greater and/or wind gusts greater or equal to 58 MPH) occurred all across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles.

A slow moving tropical wave moved into the middle Texas coast on September 14th and 15th. The tropical wave produced widespread showers and scattered thunderstorms. Periods of heavy rain caused flooding and flash flooding across the Coastal Bend both days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Farm to Market Roads 1944 and 2046 northeast of Odem were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Canadian airmass  moved into West Central Texas on December 7, dropping temperatures into the lower 30s.  Then, a deep upper trough of low pressure moved across the area early Saturday morning, resulting in snowfall amounts mostly from 1 to 3 inches along and west of a line from Bronte to Abilene to Throckmorton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Cooperative Observer measured 1.5 inches of snow at Fort Griffin State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the heels of an upper low that brought some light snow early this morning to areas such as Denver City (Yoakum County) and Brownfield (Terry County), north winds of 40 to 50 with frequent gusts over 60 mph gripped much of the region for up to ten hours in some locales.  Several dozen reports of downed trees, fallen power poles and minor structural damage were received, particularly in the Lubbock area.  These cold north winds also generated widespread blowing dust to a few miles visibility, mainly from the southern Panhandle into the northern South Plains which was coincident with a lack of snowfall and wet soil from the prior week's significant snowstorm.||The following are the maximum high wind gusts (58+ mph) per county as measured by Texas Tech University West Texas Mesonets (WTM) and ASOS stations.||Bailey: 58 mph at Muleshoe WTM|Briscoe: 66 mph at Caprock Canyon WTM|Castro: 59 mph at Friona WTM|Childress: 61 mph at Childress ASOS|Cochran: 62 mph at Morton WTM|Cottle: 60 mph at Hackberry WTM|Crosby: 65 mph at Crosbyton WTM|Garza: 59 mph at Graham WTM|Hale: 61 mph at Aiken and Abernathy WTM|Hockley: 60 mph at Levelland WTM|King: 59 mph at Guthrie WTM|Lamb: 62 mph at Olton WTM|Lubbock: 60 mph at Wolfforth WTM, 58 mph at Lubbock ASOS|Motley: 62 mph at Roaring Springs WTM|Parmer: 58 mph at Dimmitt WTM|Swisher: 64 mph at Tulia WTM|Yoakum: 62 mph at Plains WTM.

These cold north winds also generated widespread blowing dust to a few miles visibility, mainly from the southern Panhandle into the northern South Plains which was coincident with a lack of snowfall and wet soil from the prior week's significant snowstorm.||The following are the maximum high wind gusts (58+ mph) per county as measured by Texas Tech University West Texas Mesonets (WTM) and ASOS stations.||Bailey: 58 mph at Muleshoe WTM|Briscoe: 66 mph at Caprock Canyon WTM|Castro: 59 mph at Friona WTM|Childress: 61 mph at Childress ASOS|Cochran: 62 mph at Morton WTM|Cottle: 60 mph at Hackberry WTM|Crosby: 65 mph at Crosbyton WTM|Garza: 59 mph at Graham WTM|Hale: 61 mph at Aiken and Abernathy WTM|Hockley: 60 mph at Levelland WTM|King: 59 mph at Guthrie WTM|Lamb: 62 mph at Olton WTM|Lubbock: 60 mph at Wolfforth WTM, 58 mph at Lubbock ASOS|Motley: 62 mph at Roaring Springs WTM|Parmer: 58 mph at Dimmitt WTM|Swisher: 64 mph at Tulia WTM|Yoakum: 62 mph at Plains WTM.

The Grass Fire 287 & 20 wildfire began at 1345CST about three miles southeast of Lelia Lake Texas in Donley County just south of U.S. Highway 287 and then east right off of County Road 20. The wildfire consumed six hundred and forty acres and was caused by downed power lines. The wildfire began from U.S. Highway 287 to County Road X and then from County Road 20 to two-thirds of the way to County Road 22. There were no reports of structures were lost, however two homes were threatened but were saved. There was a report of pasture and some fence damage. There were no reports of any injuries or fatalities. The Clarendon VFD and the Memphis Fire Department responded to the wildfire which was contained and controlled at 1810CST.

A mid/upper level low quickly moved east across the Rolling|Plains of Texas in the afternoon hours of the 13th.  Very strong low level winds fields are the result of rapid pressure falls as the low continues to strengthen through |the evening hours. As a result, high wind criteria (sustained winds 40 MPH or greater and/or wind gusts greater or equal to 58 MPH) occurred all across the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles.
