A slow moving tropical wave moved into the middle Texas coast on September 14th and 15th. The tropical wave produced widespread showers and scattered thunderstorms. Periods of heavy rain caused flooding and flash flooding across the Coastal Bend both days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor street flooding occurred across northern portions of Corpus Christi from Morgan Street to near the intersection of Port Avenue and Crosstown Expressway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing locally heavy rainfall over Webb County caused flash flooding on the northwest side of Laredo during the early morning hours of September 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rescues were performed in northwest Laredo due to flooding near Mines Road|and Las Cruces Drive intersection.

A potent upper level low pressure system deepened across West Central Texas and brought snow mostly to higher elevations and a mix of rain and snow to lower elevations.  While most of the area experienced a trace of snow, heavy snow resulted in the closing of State Highway 70 between Sweetwater and Blackwell.  This location is about 2500 feet high in elevation.  U.S. Highway 277 also was snowpacked southwest of Abilene.  Most of the snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting to a couple of inches mainly north of a line from San Angelo to Cross Plains.  Snow began about 10 AM and continued through the evening before tapering off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About an inch of snow fell in Baird and Cross Plains.  State Highway 36 also became snow packed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active day of weather across parts of the Texas Panhandles starting with severe weather and ending with winter weather. An upper level low pressure system moved into the northwestern TX Panhandle the afternoon of the 26th. South-southeast low level flow brought moisture into the region along with favorable wind profile throughout atmosphere and sunshine breaking out allowed thunderstorms to develop. The ingredients were there to produce some severe thunderstorms with hail measured up to quarters. One thunderstorms also did produce a weak, short tracked tornado in the southeast Panhandle. On the backside of the departing low pressure system, there was strong cold air advection into the region and with good moisture aloft and at the surface coupled that with forcing, this resulted in accumulating snow for the northwest Texas Panhandle where 6-8 of snowfall fell throughout the morning of the 27th before the system exited the afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail reported just south of the Randall County feed yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain across southern portions of the Coastal Bend during the early morning hours of September 12th. The heaviest rainfall occurred over southern Jim Wells County where 6 to 8 inches of rain fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on Highway 361 near the Packery Channel bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain across southern portions of the Coastal Bend during the early morning hours of September 12th. The heaviest rainfall occurred over southern Jim Wells County where 6 to 8 inches of rain fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple videos showed street flooding near Premont via the Snapchat map. The flooding affected 25 homes in the Premont area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains across Nueces County caused major flooding along Oso Creek on the 16th. Heavy rains across the Brush Country led to flooding along the Nueces River by the latter part of the month. Nueces River reached major flood stage near Tilden on the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nueces River near Tilden reached major flood stage on the 21st. This caused major lowland flooding of livestock, roads, irrigation pumps and tank batteries. Fencing was damaged along with a few hunting cabins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains across Nueces County caused major flooding along Oso Creek on the 16th. Heavy rains across the Brush Country led to flooding along the Nueces River by the latter part of the month. Nueces River reached major flood stage near Tilden on the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains flooded Oso Creek on the 16th with the creek going above major flood stage. Major flooding reached the approaches to the Farm to Market 763 bridge. Numerous outbuildings and barns were surrounded and approached apartment buildings below Farm to Market 763.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface trough developed from the Texas Hill Country into Deep East Texas and Northcentral Louisiana during the daytime hours of December 7th, which was further enhanced as a developing warm front ahead of a strong upper level trough that translated east across much of Texas. This trough tapped into a rich supply of Pacific moisture as it spread northeast across Texas and the Lower Mississippi Valley, with strong overrunning atop this weak surface trough/warm front ahead of the upper level trough as it entered West Texas late in the day. While much of the rains during the 7th were stratoform, rainfall intensity increased during the evening and overnight hours across much of East Texas and North Louisiana, where widespread rainfall amounts of four to in excess of six inches was observed across Deep East Texas, much of North Louisiana, and extreme Southern Arkansas. These totals, atop already moist grounds from significant rains and flash flooding that was observed in Mid-November, resulted in increased runoff and flash flooding across much of Angelina County and adjacent portions of Deep East Texas, with numerous other creeks and streams rising/cresting above flood stage during the morning/afternoon hours on December 8th across East Texas, well after the heavier rains had ended. This resulted in numerous other roads across East Texas becoming flooded and closed through the remainder of the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FM 1818 at Buttermilk Road was covered in high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving tropical wave moved into the middle Texas coast on September 14th and 15th. The tropical wave produced widespread showers and scattered thunderstorms. Periods of heavy rain caused flooding and flash flooding across the Coastal Bend both days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were closed due to flooding between Odem and Taft. Farm to Market Road 1944 between U. S. Highway 77 and Sodville Road, Sodville Road between County Road 82 and Villareal Street, and Davis Road between Mexico Street and Railroad Avenue were closed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Farm to Market Road 2826 was closed between U. S. Highway 77 and Farm to Market Road 892 northeast of Robstown due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving tropical wave moved into the middle Texas coast on September 14th and 15th. The tropical wave produced widespread showers and scattered thunderstorms. Periods of heavy rain caused flooding and flash flooding across the Coastal Bend both days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pictures and videos showed flooding of streets and parking lots near Robstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain across southern portions of the Coastal Bend during the early morning hours of September 12th. The heaviest rainfall occurred over southern Jim Wells County where 6 to 8 inches of rain fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nueces County Road 28 bridge was closed between County Roads 57 and 61 due to water over the road and bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm moved over New Mexico causing strong westerly winds in the |Guadalupe Mountains, higher elevations of the Davis Mountains, and over Eddy County in |southeast New Mexico.

An upper low and a cold front generated a round of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of Wednesday, December 26. A few storms produced wind damage, primarily across the western-most counties of North Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee reported minor roof damage and a few trees down in the city of Lone Camp, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low and a cold front generated a round of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of Wednesday, December 26. A few storms produced wind damage, primarily across the western-most counties of North Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported wind damage to a metal farm shed and a mobile home along and south of Highway 391 near the city of Hearne, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late evening through early morning severe thunderstorms produced severe hail and some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed on FM 974 and Edge Cut Off Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a mild Christmas, unusually moist air streamed north early this morning complete with some thunderstorms.  This moisture deepened through the day on very breezy southeast winds all ahead of a mature upper level trough and Pacific cold front. Shortly after noon, a broken line of strong thunderstorms developed along this front across the southwest South Plains and spread swiftly east with predominantly small hail, downpours and strong wind gusts. A few of these embedded storms became severe at times with quarter size hail and high winds.  Strong low level wind shear even supported the development of a rare wintertime tornado in northern Dickens County that damaged some structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager relayed a public report of three heavily damaged silos with minor property damage to other outdoor structures about 2 miles east-northeast of Afton.  Since debris was cleaned up by the following morning, a storm survey was not possible. Once NWS personnel talked with the affected property owner who corroborated other reports of a tall funnel cloud around this time, it was deemed that a tornado was responsible for the damage. Despite no radar indication of mesocyclones with most of these non-supercell storms, other funnel clouds (and possible tornadoes) were documented on social media from similar storms later this afternoon. This tornado's event time was estimated from radar.

An upper low and a cold front generated a round of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of Wednesday, December 26. A few storms produced wind damage, primarily across the western-most counties of North Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Turbulence produced by a thunderstorm over the Dallas area injured 3 people on an aircraft inbound to Dallas, 2 of which required emergency medical care.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level trough moved into the Southern Plains during the late afternoon, evening and overnight hours of November 30th-December 1st. Upper level forcing in response to this disturbance moved into the Middle Red River Valley of Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas during the evening hours of November 30th and strong to severe thunderstorms erupted north and west of the Interstate 30 Corridor as a result. The atmosphere was moderately unstable for the last day in November but strong deep layer and low level directional shear was present. This resulted in supercell type thunderstorms and bowing thunderstorm complexes, one of which moved across Northeast Texas into Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas producing widespread wind damage all along its track. Numerous trees and power lines were downed with these storms before they exited Northeast Texas during the early morning hours of December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down near Mount Pleasant ISD.

Severe drought conditions persisted into the early part of the month over the western Brush Country. Heavy rains fell between the 10th and 20th over the region. This alleviated the drought conditions over Webb and La Salle Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the heels of an upper low that brought some light snow early this morning to areas such as Denver City (Yoakum County) and Brownfield (Terry County), north winds of 40 to 50 with frequent gusts over 60 mph gripped much of the region for up to ten hours in some locales.  Several dozen reports of downed trees, fallen power poles and minor structural damage were received, particularly in the Lubbock area.  These cold north winds also generated widespread blowing dust to a few miles visibility, mainly from the southern Panhandle into the northern South Plains which was coincident with a lack of snowfall and wet soil from the prior week's significant snowstorm.||The following are the maximum high wind gusts (58+ mph) per county as measured by Texas Tech University West Texas Mesonets (WTM) and ASOS stations.||Bailey: 58 mph at Muleshoe WTM|Briscoe: 66 mph at Caprock Canyon WTM|Castro: 59 mph at Friona WTM|Childress: 61 mph at Childress ASOS|Cochran: 62 mph at Morton WTM|Cottle: 60 mph at Hackberry WTM|Crosby: 65 mph at Crosbyton WTM|Garza: 59 mph at Graham WTM|Hale: 61 mph at Aiken and Abernathy WTM|Hockley: 60 mph at Levelland WTM|King: 59 mph at Guthrie WTM|Lamb: 62 mph at Olton WTM|Lubbock: 60 mph at Wolfforth WTM, 58 mph at Lubbock ASOS|Motley: 62 mph at Roaring Springs WTM|Parmer: 58 mph at Dimmitt WTM|Swisher: 64 mph at Tulia WTM|Yoakum: 62 mph at Plains WTM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level trough developed over the Four Corners Region on December 26th, before taking on a negative tilt as it ejected northeast across Northwest and North Texas and much of Oklahoma during the early morning hours of the 27th. Strong southerly winds ahead of this upper trough resulted in warm, humid conditions spreading north across East Texas, North Louisiana, and extreme Southern Arkansas, resulting in weak to moderate surface based instability over these areas. Wind shear was also very strong ahead of this system, with the rapidly increasing moisture return and subtle forcing ahead of the trough allowing for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and especially during the evening over East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and adjacent sections of Southwest Arkansas. Isolated supercell thunderstorms developed over portions of East Texas during the evening, with several reports of large hail and damaging winds over Smith County. Meanwhile, a squall line developed just to the west over Northern and Central Texas along the ejecting upper trough, with these thunderstorms racing east across East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, and North Louisiana during the late evening through the overnight hours of the 26th-27th. Numerous reports of damaging winds which downed trees and power lines were reported from this squall line over East Texas, before the strongest storms quickly exited this area after 1 AM on the 27th.||In addition to the damaging winds and large hail, locally heavy rainfall also accompanied these storms, with rainfall amounts ranging from two to four inches with isolated higher amounts. Much of these rains fell over an extended period of time, and given the already saturated grounds from much above normal rainfall that fell since September, many smaller creeks, streams, and bayous began to overflow their banks which flooded/closed numerous roadways across East Texas well after the heavy rainfall had ended during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Bullard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was centered near the Four Corners region which brought an increase in upper level lift across West Texas. The area was in a favorable position under the upper level jet stream which also aided in lift. A plentiful supply of moisture was in place and wind shear was favorable for thunderstorm development across West Texas. These conditions resulted in strong winds, hail, flash flooding, and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Glasscock County and produced flash flooding about 22 miles east southeast of Midland. There was a public report of six inches of water running over State Highway 137, about one mile north of State Highway 158.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain of two to four inches fell across the Upper Sabine Basin of Northeast Texas on December 26th-27th, atop already saturated grounds where five to seven inches fell during December. This resulted in moderate flooding developing along the Sabine River near Mineola, as well as along Lake Fork Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall of two to four inches fell across the Upper Sabine River Basin of Northeast Texas on December 26th-27th, atop already saturated grounds where rainfall amounts of five to seven inches fell during the month of December. The associated runoff resulted in the Sabine River near Mineola to rise above its 14 foot flood stage by 730 am on the 27th, where it neared the 18 foot moderate flood stage around 9 am on the 30th. This resulted in FM 1804 from the Smith/Wood County line north to Highway 80 to be closed due to flooding. ||In addition, Lake Fork Creek just southwest of Quitman also rose above its 16 foot flood stage around 10 pm on the 27th, and neared the 18 foot moderate flood stage around 2 pm on the 28th, before falling back to near flood stage by midnight on the 31st. This resulted in FM 1254 being closed due to flooding at the Lake Fork Creek bridge between  Loop 564 near Mineola and FM 778 southeast of Quitman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across the Four State Region during the predawn hours of December 14th in response to a vigorous, closed upper level low pressure system across Central and Southeast Texas. At the surface, an occluded frontal boundary was located across Northern Louisiana and some surface based instability was |located along this boundary. Very cold temperatures aloft contributed to steep lapse rates throughout the atmosphere and the end result were several hail producing thunderstorms.  Hail ranged from as small as pea size to as large as ping pong ball sized across portions of Northeast Texas for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell near the Atlanta State Park on Lake Wright Patman.

Hail ranged from as small as pea size to as large as ping pong ball sized across portions of Northeast Texas for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail fell along Highway 67 in the Redwater community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving tropical wave moved into the middle Texas coast on September 14th and 15th. The tropical wave produced widespread showers and scattered thunderstorms. Periods of heavy rain caused flooding and flash flooding across the Coastal Bend both days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were impassable due to flash flooding near the waterfront at Ingleside on the Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level trough developed over the Four Corners Region on December 26th, before taking on a negative tilt as it ejected northeast across Northwest and North Texas and much of Oklahoma during the early morning hours of the 27th. Strong southerly winds ahead of this upper trough resulted in warm, humid conditions spreading north across East Texas, North Louisiana, and extreme Southern Arkansas, resulting in weak to moderate surface based instability over these areas. Wind shear was also very strong ahead of this system, with the rapidly increasing moisture return and subtle forcing ahead of the trough allowing for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and especially during the evening over East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and adjacent sections of Southwest Arkansas. Isolated supercell thunderstorms developed over portions of East Texas during the evening, with several reports of large hail and damaging winds over Smith County. Meanwhile, a squall line developed just to the west over Northern and Central Texas along the ejecting upper trough, with these thunderstorms racing east across East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, and North Louisiana during the late evening through the overnight hours of the 26th-27th. Numerous reports of damaging winds which downed trees and power lines were reported from this squall line over East Texas, before the strongest storms quickly exited this area after 1 AM on the 27th.||In addition to the damaging winds and large hail, locally heavy rainfall also accompanied these storms, with rainfall amounts ranging from two to four inches with isolated higher amounts. Much of these rains fell over an extended period of time, and given the already saturated grounds from much above normal rainfall that fell since September, many smaller creeks, streams, and bayous began to overflow their banks which flooded/closed numerous roadways across East Texas well after the heavy rainfall had ended during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a power line at the 11600 block of County Road 246 South.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper low moved east across the northern Permian Basin late in the night on Friday before exiting the region Saturday afternoon. Northwest of this low, a large deformation zone developed accompanied by moderate to heavy snow.  At the height of the storm around 6 AM Saturday, snowfall rates were measured as great as 2 inches per hour by NWS employees. The result was a large swath of heavy wet snow between 4 and 10.5 inches spanning 16 counties across the South Plains and Rolling Plains, with lesser amounts elsewhere.||Snow covered and icy roads severely hampered travel over the region through the weekend and resulted in sizable economic losses, particularly for some Lubbock businesses. Some roads such as the South Loop 289 flyover were closed due to hazardous ice buildup.  Hundreds of minor auto accidents were reported in Lubbock County alone, with similar numbers occurring Saturday night through Sunday night following refreezing of water on area roads and overpasses. Monetary losses were not available at the time of this entry.||This snowstorm was unique in that many weather models leading up to the event severely failed to capture the magnitude and extent of the snowfall. The official forecast favored a low impact event with 1 to 4 inches of total snowfall. Much of the rationale for low snow amounts was attributed to the upper low forecast to weaken and shear apart as it passed just south of the region. However, the upper low remained intact and even slowed its eastward progress thereby prolonging the duration of moderate to heavy snow over much of the South Plains.  The high water content of this snow proved beneficial in restoring soil moisture to much of the region.||********************STORM TOTAL SNOWFALL (inches)********************||...Bailey County...| A slow moving upper low moved east across the northern Permian Basin late in the night on Friday before exiting the region Saturday afternoon. Hundreds of minor auto accidents were reported in Lubbock County alone, with similar numbers occurring Saturday night through Sunday night following refreezing of water on area roads and overpasses. Monetary losses were not available at the time of this entry.||This snowstorm was unique in that many weather models leading up to the event severely failed to capture the magnitude and extent of the snowfall. The official forecast favored a low impact event with 1 to 4 inches of total snowfall. Much of the rationale for low snow amounts was attributed to the upper low forecast to weaken and shear apart as it passed just south of the region. However, the upper low remained intact and even slowed its eastward progress thereby prolonging the duration of moderate to heavy snow over much of the South Plains. Dec 1: The 0000168 Wildfire began 1355CST about six miles southwest of Conlen Texas in Dallam County off US Highway 54 East. The wildfire consumed three hundred and seventy-nine acres and was caused by equipment use. The Dalhart VFD responded to the wildfire which was controlled at 1644CST. There were no reports of any injuries or fatalities. There were also no reports of any homes or other structures damaged or lost from the wildfire.

A vigorous upper level trough developed over the Four Corners Region on December 26th, before taking on a negative tilt as it ejected northeast across Northwest and North Texas and much of Oklahoma during the early morning hours of the 27th. Strong southerly winds ahead of this upper trough resulted in warm, humid conditions spreading north across East Texas, North Louisiana, and extreme Southern Arkansas, resulting in weak to moderate surface based instability over these areas. Wind shear was also very strong ahead of this system, with the rapidly increasing moisture return and subtle forcing ahead of the trough allowing for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and especially during the evening over East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and adjacent sections of Southwest Arkansas. Isolated supercell thunderstorms developed over portions of East Texas during the evening, with several reports of large hail and damaging winds over Smith County. Meanwhile, a squall line developed just to the west over Northern and Central Texas along the ejecting upper trough, with these thunderstorms racing east across East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, and North Louisiana during the late evening through the overnight hours of the 26th-27th. Numerous reports of damaging winds which downed trees and power lines were reported from this squall line over East Texas, before the strongest storms quickly exited this area after 1 AM on the 27th.||In addition to the damaging winds and large hail, locally heavy rainfall also accompanied these storms, with rainfall amounts ranging from two to four inches with isolated higher amounts. Much of these rains fell over an extended period of time, and given the already saturated grounds from much above normal rainfall that fell since September, many smaller creeks, streams, and bayous began to overflow their banks which flooded/closed numerous roadways across East Texas well after the heavy rainfall had ended during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on FM 144 near Cason.

Widespread heavy rainfall across the Neches River basin of East Texas to start the final week of December resulted in moderate flooding along the Neches River near Alto.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall amounts of two to four inches fell across the Neches River Basin of East Texas on December 26th-27th, atop already saturated grounds where six to ten inches of rain had fallen throughout the month of December. As a result, the Neches River southwest of Alto near Highway 21 rose above the 16 foot flood stage at 7 am on December 27th, reached the 18 foot moderate flood stage at 1115 pm on the 27th, and crested at 18.45 feet at 445 am on the 28th. The Neches River remained near 18 feet for the remainder of December, with a new and higher crest occurring after the first of the new year in 2019 due to additional rains that had fallen. ||This flooding resulted in moderate to severe flooding of the heavily wooded floodplain, with boat ramps and picnic areas completely inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level low pressure system deepened across West Central Texas and brought snow mostly to higher elevations and a mix of rain and snow to lower elevations.  While most of the area experienced a trace of snow, heavy snow resulted in the closing of State Highway 70 between Sweetwater and Blackwell.  This location is about 2500 feet high in elevation.  U.S. Highway 277 also was snowpacked southwest of Abilene.  Most of the snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting to a couple of inches mainly north of a line from San Angelo to Cross Plains.  Snow began about 10 AM and continued through the evening before tapering off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow covered the hills in northern Coke County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level disturbance and a Pacific Cold front interacted with a moist and unstable airmass across the Big Country or the region along and north of Interstate 20.  Surface dewpoints were in the upper 60s.  A line of thunderstorms developed and resulted in several damaging thunderstorm wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Texas Department of Transportation Abilene District reported a large highway sign blown down along the Winters Freeway.

A cold Canadian airmass  moved into West Central Texas on December 7, dropping temperatures into the lower 30s.  Then, a deep upper trough of low pressure moved across the area early Saturday morning, resulting in snowfall amounts mostly from 1 to 3 inches along and west of a line from Bronte to Abilene to Throckmorton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Cooperative Observer measured 3 inches of snow in Abilene.  This is also an all time record snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad tropical wave originating in the western Caribbean Sea moved across the Yucatan peninsula on June 15th and 16th, 2018. The slow moving but persistent system approached the Texas coast on Sunday, June 17th before finally arriving on June 18th. As the center axis of the wave passed the longitude of the coastline, the gates opened to repeated surges of tropical moisture deep into the atmosphere with origins in the western Caribbean Sea. A series of upper level disturbances interacted with this wave throughout its life cycle and helped to maintain a broad area of low pressure at all levels of the atmosphere for the period from June 18th through June 22nd across Rio Grande Valley, and the adjacent Gulf waters. Several surface-based boundaries and convergence along the coastline also aided the production of heavy rainfall. Individual convective systems spun in place at several points during the event, including:||*June 19 (Tuesday morning) in Brooks County|*June 19 (Tuesday afternoon) in Cameron County|*June 20 (Early Wednesday) in Hidalgo, Cameron, and Willacy County|*June 20 (Early afternoon) again in Cameron and Willacy County|*June 21 (Thursday morning) in Starr and Hidalgo County|*June 21 (Thursday afternoon) in Cameron, Willacy, and Kenedy County|*June 22 (Friday morning) in southwest Hidalgo and southern Starr County along the Rio Grande.||The wave and upper level disturbances finally exited southwest into north central Mexico on June 22nd, bringing a weekend of rain-free but oppressively hot and humid conditions to conduct broad recovery activity across the region.||In the larger population centers of Hidalgo County, as well as around Harlingen and Los Fresnos in Cameron County, between 12 and 18 inches of rainfall fell.  Scenes of several feet of water that flooded thousands of vehicles, inundated several thousand homes and businesses with 18 inches to more than 3 feet of water, and hundreds of rescues of persons and pets were common. As of June 25, Emergency Declarations (state) were declared for Cameron, Hidalgo, and Willacy County. ||As of mid August, the current statistics for the Hidalgo, Cameron, and Willacy portion of the affected area were as follows:||*More than 7,400 residences and businesses in Cameron and Willacy County with flood damage defined as minor to destructive by FEMA Standards.|*At least 20,000 residences and businesses considered affected by the floods. This includes the 7,400 listed above.|*More than 600 persons in at least ten shelters at the peak of the area-wide flooding|*Several thousand vehicles with varying degrees of flood damage|*Dozens to hundreds of roads, from neighborhood streets to major thoroughfares and frontage roads, closed during and after peak flooding|*More than 2,000 rescues from vehicles and homes were conducted|*21,000 meals served by the American Red Cross|*Public infrastructure damage estimated to be $50 million in Hidalgo and Willacy County alone||The breadth of the impact was greater than that of Hurricane Dolly in 2008, and for many in Hidalgo County, the rainfall totals ended up between Dolly's and Beulah's total.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple cells and clusters of thunderstorms during the pre-dawn hours into the morning commute on June 20th dropped measured and estimated rainfall between 5 and more than 8 inches in western Cameron County, which caused widespread flooding between La Feria, Harlingen, San Benito, and other locations. The flooding produced locally 2 to 4 feet of water depth, with Santa Rosa through La Feria particularly hard hit. Interstate 2 frontage road was closed from Harlingen to McAllen due to the intense rainfall, which fell at an estimated rate of 2 to 3 inches per hour in a few spots for 2 to 3 hours - and on top of prior rainfall earlier in the week.||Detailed damage for this particular event was not known, though total damage through multiple agencies along with standard doubling for uninsured properties, as well as significant damage to drainage canals, suggested event totals in Cameron County were over $60 million.  For this particular event, which included some of the highest rainfall rates, damage was estimated at around $35 million.  There were an unknown number of properties and vehicles damaged in this particular flood, though the numbers are likely above 1,000 for each case.  ||Full details will be available within 12 to 18 months of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical depression had made landfall over Baja, California and was moving across northern Mexico within upper level southwest winds. A cold front was moving across West Texas. An abundance of moisture was present, and upper level lift increased across the region during the evening and overnight of October 12th as the tropical depression came closer. These conditions resulted in hail, strong winds, and flash flooding across West Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Upton County and produced a 69 mph wind gust in McCamey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was centered near the Four Corners region which brought an increase in upper level lift across West Texas. The area was in a favorable position under the upper level jet stream which also aided in lift. A plentiful supply of moisture was in place and wind shear was favorable for thunderstorm development across West Texas. These conditions resulted in strong winds, hail, flash flooding, and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Reagan County and produced a 62 mph wind gust about five miles southwest of St. Lawrence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was centered near the Four Corners region which brought an increase in upper level lift across West Texas. The area was in a favorable position under the upper level jet stream which also aided in lift. A plentiful supply of moisture was in place and wind shear was favorable for thunderstorm development across West Texas. These conditions resulted in strong winds, hail, flash flooding, and a tornado.

The remnants of Tropical Storm Sergio from the Eastern Pacific quickly translated east across the Red River Valley of Southern Oklahoma and North Texas during the morning and afternoon hours of October 13th. This resulted in a strong surface low development over Westcentral Texas during the morning hours, which allowed for a warm front to mix its way north across Northcentral and Northeast Texas to along the Arkansas/Louisiana border by afternoon. Large scale forcing increased during the afternoon and early evening over Northeast Texas where moderate instability had developed near the warm front and ahead of the remnants of Sergio, which resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms developing along and north of the warm front. Strong low level speed and directional shear developed southeast of the surface low and warm front over Northeast Texas, which allowed for several mini-supercell thunderstorms to develop and become severe. These storms produced several instances of damaging winds and large hail, and also an isolated tornado in Western Panola County southwest of Beckville. These storms weakened by the mid evening hours with increasing stability with the loss of heating, and the departure of forcing aloft associated with the remnants of Sergio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down across Kilgore.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado with maximum estimated winds near 85 mph touched down along FM 1251 breaking large branches, where it moved northeast and crossed County Road 237. It uprooted a few trees and broke large branches before moving to the east-northeast where it crossed County Road 241. The tornado caused the most damage here where it uprooted several trees and snapped several large branches. The tornado continued moving to the northeast and intersected an unnamed mining road where it snapped several large branches before finally lifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning showers and thunderstorms across much of the South Plains were followed by additional rounds of heavy rainfall through midday.  The latter rounds of rain were focused near and just east of the Interstate 27 and US Highway 87 corridor where as much as two inches of rain fell on top of soils that were already saturated from heavy rains that fell just three days earlier. The result was widespread flooding of poor drainage areas over much of the South Plains, including a few temporary road closures such as FM Road 179 near Cotton Center where nearly three feet water covered the road.  In the wake of the flooding rains, a supercell thunderstorm developed in western Terry County along an outflow boundary leftover from the morning rain.  After producing a five-minute tornado west of Brownfield over open land, this storm crawled east for the next 1.5 hours with severe hail at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved through a slow moving trough over the southwestern US and triggered thunderstorms over South Central Texas. Storms were fed by a warm, moist boundary layer and some reached severe strength and produced locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain that led to flooding of the Sabinal River. At Sabinal the river crested at 15.34 feet late on 10/9/18. Flood stage is 12 feet. It dropped below flood stage early on the 10th. A truck was washed away on RR 187 at a low water crossing that remained flooded south of Sabinal at 1:50 am Oct 12. A man in his 20s was killed and his body was found approximately one mile from the river crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a very moist atmosphere and generated showers and thunderstorms some of which produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding. Rainfall totals during this episode included 8.40 inches at Mountain Home in Kerr County, 6.26 inches at Llano, and 9.27 inches at Castell in Llano County.  This widespread rainfall created a massive flood along the Llano River and its nearby creeks.  The flood waters went into the Colorado River and produced lake flooding on Lake LBJ, Lake Marble Falls, and Lake Travis where hundreds of homes were flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding closing RR 335 along the Nueces River north of Barksdale.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: After 5-10 inches of rain fell in the Llano River basin flood waters moved into the Colorado River basin along with heavy rain in the basin. Lake Travis rose into its flood pool (681 feet MSL) on the 16th and eventually rose to 704.3 feet MSL on the 19th. This flood water overwhelmed Austin's water treatment facilities and the city issued a boil water notice on the 22nd. The city and Travis and Williamson Counties distributed bottled water to residents. The Lower Colorado River Authority conducted flood operations opening as many as eight floodgates on Mansfield Dam to reduce the level of the lake. By the 28th the level was sufficiently low to allow the water treatment facilities to operate normally and the boil water notice was lifted.  In and around the Lake Travis area there were reportedly nearly 100 homes that experienced Major damage, 76 with Minor damage, and over 200 homes that were Affected.   Damage estimates and insured losses are not complete as of this writing but will likely top 10 million dollars.  The monetary loss to businesses in Austin with regards to the boil water notice are unknown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain overnight produced flash flooding that washed away the FM 2900 bridge across the Llano River in Kingsland. A body was recovered near Kingsland and it was determined that this fatality came from a flood in the city of Junction the previous week.  Damage to the bridge is estimated at 20 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms over Central and Southeast Texas produced localized wind damage over Anderson County before moving southeast of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a couple of large trees blown down and a steeple blown off of a church.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the course of several days, a dynamic weather system produced severe weather across North and Central Texas. Several low-end tornadoes developed, causing damage to several properties on the 13th. Additional severe storms occurred on the 14th, with mostly large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado landed just southwest of Waxahachie High School where a convergent path was noted in grass. Damage occurred to a window and bay door on the gymnasium corner. The tornado continued northeast for a little longer breaking out windows in cars and sliding them at an angle. Three parking lot lights were bent to the ground, and 3 handicap signs were also pushed down to the ground along the path. Finally a small empty school band trailer was picked up and tossed into the nearby field about 50 yards from its original location. The maximum estimated winds were 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the course of several days, a dynamic weather system produced severe weather across North and Central Texas. Several low-end tornadoes developed, causing damage to several properties on the 13th. Additional severe storms occurred on the 14th, with mostly large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The survey team found damage consistent with an EF0 tornado, with peak winds of 80 mph. A few trees and homes were damaged south of downtown Hillsboro (approximately 4 miles south) on the west side of Interstate 35. The tornado damage was localized to a small neighborhood near Patton Mill Rd. Mostly shingles and fences were damaged at these residences. The tornado dissipated before crossing I-35. The maximum estimated winds were 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a very moist atmosphere and generated showers and thunderstorms some of which produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain mainly west of Burnet County and lakes LBJ and Marble Falls, caused rapid rises on the Colorado River.  Several hundred thousand CFS of flood water entered Lake LBJ near Kingsland (where the Llano River enters the lake), with flood waters flowing down the Colorado River into Lake Marble Falls.  Lake LBJ and Marble Falls rose rapidly, flooding homes and some businesses.  While the lake only rose a few feet at Wirtz Dam, farther upstream on Lake LBJ near Kingsland, Granite Shoals, Highland Haven, and Cottonwood shores, rapid rises on the Lake caused many homes to flood.  Lake LBJ started to rise in the predawn hours and continued high through the day.  Downstream of Wirtz dam is Lake Marble Falls which saw a 15 feet rise in less than 12 hours.  Evacuation notices were sent out near the Lake on the morning of Oct 16.  For the above mentioned communities, estimates of damage include 17 homes destroyed, 168 with Major damage, 64 homes with Minor Damage, and 31 homes that were affected.  As of this writing, early and ongoing flood insurance payouts totaled over 11 million dollars so far along with roughly 7 million spend on debris removal and 2 million dollars worth of damage to the Marble Falls water treatment plant.  Additional flood repairs to homes should easily surpass an additional 10-15 million dollars when you total the several hundred homes impacted.  It is estimated that total losses in Burnet County and Lakes LBJ and Marble Falls could surpass 30 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a very moist atmosphere and generated showers and thunderstorms some of which produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding. Rainfall totals during this episode included 8.40 inches at Mountain Home in Kerr County, 6.26 inches at Llano, and 9.27 inches at Castell in Llano County.  This widespread rainfall created a massive flood along the Llano River and its nearby creeks.  The flood waters went into the Colorado River and produced lake flooding on Lake LBJ, Lake Marble Falls, and Lake Travis where hundreds of homes were flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain across Llano County and points west produced a large flash flood on the Llano River.  The flash flood emptied into Lake LBJ at Kingland, causing water to back up in the lake and flood areas as early as 7 am north of Kingland.  Many homes along the canals had 4-6 feet of water in them.  Many homes along Lake LBJ in Kingsland flooded, as well as areas down the lake at Sunrise Beach and Horseshoe Bay.  Lake LBJ started to rise in the predawn hours and continued high through the day.  In Llano County, preliminary damage assessments show estimates of 38 homes destroyed, 494 with Major damage, 140 with minor damage, and 49 that were affected.  A few businesses also took on water.  The FM 2900 highway bridge was destroyed with a total replacement cost estimated to be 20 million dollars.  As of this writing, over 8 million has been paid out to Flood insurance claims with much more to likely come.  Infrastructure loses including debris removal should surpass 12 million dollars.  Home damage could easily be estimated at an additional 15 to 20 million dollars.  As of this writing will place a damage estimate of 50 million although numbers could exceed this.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent onshore flow and high astronomical tides caused periodic flooding of beaches along the middle Texas coast from the 8th until the 11th. Swells from Hurricane Micheal reached the middle Texas coast on the 12th. The swells pushed water levels to 3 feet above mean sea level during the afternoon and evening of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water reaching the dunes closed the beach access road at Padre Balli Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The survey team determined a second tornado occurred on the east side of I-35, a little over 3.5 miles southeast of downtown Hillsboro. Tornado damage was first seen at the end of HCR 3267 where a few trees were damaged and one home lost its roof deck. Then, this tornado produced damage consistent with an EF1 wind speed of 100 mph on HCR 3267 where one home lost a substantial portion of the roof, deck, walls and windows. The tornado destroyed a small storage shed, and it also damaged a RV trailer and small boat. The RV and the boat were picked up and tossed a few yards from their original location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the course of several days, a dynamic weather system produced severe weather across North and Central Texas. Several low-end tornadoes developed, causing damage to several properties on the 13th. Additional severe storms occurred on the 14th, with mostly large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst caused scattered tree damage in the Shiloh area of Limestone County. Several large tree branches were knocked down and some large trees were broken across the trunk. One metal barn was destroyed. Winds were estimated at 70-75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the course of several days, a dynamic weather system produced severe weather across North and Central Texas. Several low-end tornadoes developed, causing damage to several properties on the 13th. Additional severe storms occurred on the 14th, with mostly large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of damage. The emergency manager reported a storage unit facility was damaged between Eureka and Mildred but an exact location could not be obtained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent onshore flow and high astronomical tides caused periodic flooding of beaches along the middle Texas coast from the 8th until the 11th. Swells from Hurricane Micheal reached the middle Texas coast on the 12th. The swells pushed water levels to 3 feet above mean sea level during the afternoon and evening of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water reaching the dunes closed the beach access road at Padre Balli Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across Southeast Texas spawning multiple tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado began just west of the Neches River then moved into Jasper County. Damage was done to a few trees before moving across the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across Southeast Texas spawning multiple tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado started just west of the Neches river and moved east for a time just north of HWY 96. The tornado moved slightly northeast damaging several roofs of homes and blowing out the walls of a metal building. Several trees were also snapped along the path. The tornado crossed HWY 96 north of Evadale and ended near county road 847. The maximum estimate winds were 107 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado briefly touched down south of Buna along county road 784 producing minor tree damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado was observed by a storm spotter near the intersection of Hwy 31 and FM 744 to the southwest of Corsicana. Picture evidence was shared of the tornado but no additional details were provided, and the location of the tornado is estimated. The time is also estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the course of several days, a dynamic weather system produced severe weather across North and Central Texas. Several low-end tornadoes developed, causing damage to several properties on the 13th. Additional severe storms occurred on the 14th, with mostly large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser/spotter observed a tornado near FM 1243 north-|northeast of Bynum. The tornado was partially obscured by rain. No|damage could be found or accessed by a storm survey team and local|officials, but some tree damage was reported very near the assumed |track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system deepened over the Southwest U.S. before advancing east into the Plains. This system was accompanied by a slow-moving cold front, and together produced multiple days of showers and thunderstorms across the region. A few severe storms and additional flooding occurred in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Friars Creek in the South Temple Community Park was reported to be out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front lifted north across the South Plains early this afternoon before eventually intersecting a surface trough in the far southwest Texas Panhandle. As storms developed along the trough, they moved north and acquired strong low level rotation as they interacted with the warm front.  One of these supercell storms produced a large cone tornado in far northwest Castro County that fortunately remained over farm land producing no known damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was initially reported to Castro County Police by a motorist near Dimmitt.  With time, this initially small tornado grew into a large cone as it moved north at about 30 mph.  No known damage accompanied this tornado as it remained over farm land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and moderate instability fueled the development of scattered to numerous thunderstorms late this Friday afternoon through the overnight.  In addition to disrupting and cancelling many area high school football games, some of these storms became severe with high winds and large hail.  Torrential downpours also resulted in some flooding, especially in and around the Lubbock area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down at 34th Street and Globe Avenue in eastern Lubbock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a very moist atmosphere and generated showers and thunderstorms some of which produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding. Rainfall totals during this episode included 8.40 inches at Mountain Home in Kerr County, 6.26 inches at Llano, and 9.27 inches at Castell in Llano County.  This widespread rainfall created a massive flood along the Llano River and its nearby creeks.  The flood waters went into the Colorado River and produced lake flooding on Lake LBJ, Lake Marble Falls, and Lake Travis where hundreds of homes were flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused an extensive rise in area creeks and rivers, pushing the Llano River to near 40 feet at the USGS river gauge in Llano.  This is the 2nd highest ever recorded height on the river in the city of Llano.  Flow peaked out about 285,000 CFS around 730 AM that morning nearly coming up to the level of the Highway 16 (Ford St.) bridge in town.  Several homes were flooded along the river with damage to the area parks as well.  A 58 year old woman lost her life in her vehicle as she was trying to get to work in town near West Sandstone and Flag Street.  Time when she entered the water is unknown but is estimated around 5 AM that morning.  Phone and internet service went down as the high water flow washed away phone lines underneath the Llano River Bridge.  Damage in the city of Llano will likely surpass 1 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across Southeast Texas spawning multiple tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado started along FM 253 causing roof damage to one home. The tornado then moved through a remote area causing tree damage for several miles to the northeast before ending near HWY 87 in Newton County. The maximum estimated winds were 104 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across Southeast Texas spawning multiple tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado moved out of Jasper County and across forested land in Newton County. The tornado dissipated near Highway 87. The maximum estimated winds were 104 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even with a fairly wet month of October, little change occurred as far as drought conditions across eastern Hudspeth County due to the extended period of below normal precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe(D2) drought continued through October for the northeastern half of the zone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The eastern half of the zone remained in Severe(D2) drought through the month of October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the course of several days, a dynamic weather system produced severe weather across North and Central Texas. Several low-end tornadoes developed, causing damage to several properties on the 13th. Additional severe storms occurred on the 14th, with mostly large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cone-shaped tornado was observed and videoed by storm chasers|and storm spotters to the west of Interstate 45 north of Buffalo|and Donie in Freestone County. The tornado became rain wrapped and|difficult to see, but is not believed to have crossed Interstate|45. The tornado occurred over a mostly rural area with limited |road access, but some tree damage was observed near the end of the|tornado track. Power flashes were also observed in a spotter |video.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early October saw an exceptionally moist and unsettled pattern grip much of West Texas.  The ensuing periodic rounds of rainfall produced generally between 3 and 6 inches of rain over much of the South Plains, Rolling Plains and southern Texas Panhandle. Additional heavy rains fell on the 12th and 13th thanks to the remnants of Tropical Storm Sergio from the eastern Pacific.  All six counties that began the month with D2 conditions (severe drought) saw drought levels fall to D0 (abnormally dry) to D1 (moderate drought) following the prolonged cool and abnormally wet conditions. Unfortunately, many cotton farmers reported water damage and degradation to their cotton fibers following the many rounds of rain. For those farmers whose cotton bolls had not yet fully matured, a prolonged and early freeze across all of the South Plains on October 15th dealt further damage.  The USDA estimated that about 1/2 of the regional cotton crop (or just over 1 million cotton bales) would have to be abandoned this year due to combined effects from the severe spring and summer drought, heavy rains in the Fall, and an early season freeze.  Estimates indicate the drought alone inflicted nearly $400 million in combined losses for cotton, sorghum, corn, and peanut farming.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Texas Tech University West Texas mesonet site near Plains measured a wind gust to 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the course of several days, a dynamic weather system produced severe weather across North and Central Texas. Several low-end tornadoes developed, causing damage to several properties on the 13th. Additional severe storms occurred on the 14th, with mostly large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout was observed over Richland Chambers Lake between the FM 2859 bridge and Hwy 287. It was reported the waterspout may have started as a tornado on the west shore of the lake, but this could be not confirmed and no damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough over the southwestern US moved slowly toward the east causing heights to fall over Texas. A shortwave trough moved through this pattern and triggered thunderstorms across the western part of South Central Texas. Southeasterly low level flow ahead of this disturbance had increased precipitable water values to around 1.8 inches which was sufficient to produce locally heavy rain that led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding in Camp Wood near the Nueces River. An observer reported 4.81 inches of rain in Camp Wood.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaches were closed at Padre Island National Seashore due to coastal flooding with water reaching the dunes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved through a slow moving trough over the southwestern US and triggered thunderstorms over South Central Texas. Storms were fed by a warm, moist boundary layer and some reached severe strength and produced locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding closing Hwy 55 near Pulliam Creek between Rocksprings and Barksdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved through a slow moving trough over the southwestern US and triggered thunderstorms over South Central Texas. Storms were fed by a warm, moist boundary layer and some reached severe strength and produced locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a wind gust measured at 72 mph by the ASOS at Laughlin AFB. There was also 3/4 inch hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced wind gusts estimated at 60 mph that knocked down some large tree limbs along FM 2804 northwest of Brackettville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced golf ball size hail near Montell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted through western north Texas on the morning of the 7th, producing heavy rain and isolated flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple intersections with water flowing over them. There was at least one high water rescue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of episodes of locally heavy rain on the 7th and 15th over the Upper Nueces River watershed provided periods of major flooding on the Nueces River in La Salle County during the month of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall between 4 to 6 inches occurred on October 7th across the Upper Nueces River watershed. The flow from this heavy rainfall caused the Nueces River at Cotulla to reach major flood stage on the 15th and continued through the 19th across La Salle County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of episodes of locally heavy rain on the 7th and 15th over the Upper Nueces River watershed provided periods of major flooding on the Nueces River in La Salle County during the month of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall between 5 to 8 inches occurred on October 15th across the Upper Nueces River watershed. The flow from this heavy rainfall caused the Nueces River at Cotulla to reach major flood stage on the 20th and continued through the 25th across La Salle County. Major and massive lowland flooding occurred. Many roads near the river were flooded, including Farm to Market Road 3408 from Interstate 35, Valley Wells Road, and the frontage road near mile marker 67. Flooding also occurred on Dobie Road including in and around Farm to Market Road 624. FM 624 flooded south of Highway 97 toward Fowlerton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of episodes of locally heavy rain on the 7th and 15th over the Upper Nueces River watershed provided periods of major flooding on the Nueces River. The flood wave reached McMullen County during the latter half of the month of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood wave reached McMullen County on the 20th from the heavy rainfall that occurred over the upper Nueces River watershed. Major flooding continued until the end of the month. Major flooding from west of Tilden to south of Calliham occurred. Roads and bridges near the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flood wave from heavy rainfall over the upper Nueces River watershed reached Live Oak County by the end of the month. Major flooding occurred from west of Three Rivers to near George West. Releases from Lake Corpus Christi produced major flooding on the Nueces River downstream from the dam to the Calallen area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding occurred from west of Three Rivers to near George West. Flow backed up into several minor creeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies in the southwest Texas Panhandle allowed thunderstorms to rapidly develop across that region. With weak steering winds under 20 MPH, storms slowly drifted to the northeast mainly staying over parts of Deaf Smith, Oldham, Potter, and Randall counties. Some cores did develop severe hail and did exhibit strong rotation. The main with these storms were the back building and training of thunderstorms that resulted in flash flooding and numerous flooding reports across the aforementioned counties that were still occurring into the morning hours on the 8th. In a time frame of 24-48 hours after the event, several reports from the public and media showed numerous areas around the Bushland area still under a foot or two of still/moving water covering roadways and impacting some barns/outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Underpass of I-40 at Bell Street is flooded and closed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Releases from Wesley Seale Dam pushed the Nueces River into major flood stream downstream from the dam, affecting San Patricio County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning showers and thunderstorms across much of the South Plains were followed by additional rounds of heavy rainfall through midday.  The latter rounds of rain were focused near and just east of the Interstate 27 and US Highway 87 corridor where as much as two inches of rain fell on top of soils that were already saturated from heavy rains that fell just three days earlier. The result was widespread flooding of poor drainage areas over much of the South Plains, including a few temporary road closures such as FM Road 179 near Cotton Center where nearly three feet water covered the road.  In the wake of the flooding rains, a supercell thunderstorm developed in western Terry County along an outflow boundary leftover from the morning rain.  After producing a five-minute tornado west of Brownfield over open land, this storm crawled east for the next 1.5 hours with severe hail at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado drifted slowly east over cotton fields.  Damage, if any, was not known.

A southwest to northeast band of persistent moderate to at times heavy rainfall developed during the early evening hours on October 15th across portions of Central Angelina and Southeast Nacogdoches Counties in Deep East Texas, as strong overrunning began atop a cool, shallow air mass in wake of a cold frontal passage that moved through earlier in the day. The rain rates were enhanced by late evening through the early morning hours on October 16th, where strong elevated forcing occurred near embedded upper level disturbances in the deep and very moist southwest flow aloft. This resulted in widespread areas of four to seven inches of rain across Central Angelina and Southeast Nacogdoches Counties, which resulted in multiple roads being covered in water and/or closed across Southern and Central Angelina County during the early morning hours on October 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FM 1818 at Buttermilk Road east of Diboll, FM 324 at the Sewer Treatment Plant just south of Lufkin, FM 2497 at Highway 59 in Diboll, and the intersection of Old Union Road and Mount Carmel Road in Hudson were all closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed across the western Panhandles and as one of thunderstorms moved towards southern Amarillo, an outflow boundary developed out ahead of it. A Special Weather Statement was issued for wind gusts up to 55 MPH as indicated by the velocities on the radar. The storm peak wind gust however did reach up to 59 MPH as reported by a mesonet station.

An upper level trough amplified during the daytime hours of October 31st across much of the Plains and Southern Rockies, which reinforced a strong cold front southeast into the Southern Plains. Ahead of this front and accompanying trough, above normal temperatures ranging from the mid 70s to lower 80s coupled with increased low level moisture resulted in a moderately unstable air mass during the afternoon. The pressure gradient tightened across the warm sector which expanded across much of the Ark-La-Tex, well ahead of the front as surface low pressure intensified along the frontal surface over North Texas. This enhanced wind shear as the front advanced east across much of Texas and Southeast Oklahoma, with large scale forcing ahead of the trough contributing to scattered shower and thunderstorm development during the afternoon over Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, and Central Texas. Some of these storms became severe of portions of Southwest Arkansas and extreme Northeast Texas, with a squall line developing farther west over Central Texas by mid and late afternoon, which quickly advanced east across much of East Texas and North Louisiana during the late afternoon and evening hours. Several reports of wind damage which downed trees and power lines were received across East Texas and Northwest Louisiana, with even an isolated tornado having touched down in the community of Cushing in Northwest Nacogdoches County. This squall line weakened as it progressed east into Northcentral Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed on County Road 4201 in the Rocky Branch community.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado with maximum estimated winds near 107 mph touched down on the southwest side of the community of Cushing, in a forested area west of 6th Street. This tornado tracked northeast on the south and east side of Cushing, where it picked up and flipped both a trampoline and an above ground pool while damaging the frame of a mobile home. The most focused damage was along the corner of Eucalyptus Avenue and 4th Street, where several trees were uprooted or snapped. As the tornado continued northeast, it tore off half of a sheet metal roof of a house as it crossed Highway 204, while also snapping a cross of a power pole. Additional trees were snapped or uprooted before the tornado lifted just north of Highway 204 on the northeast side of Cushing. In all, 16 structures were damaged and approximately 50 trees were snapped or uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Sergio from the Eastern Pacific quickly translated east across the Red River Valley of Southern Oklahoma and North Texas during the morning and afternoon hours of October 13th. This resulted in a strong surface low development over Westcentral Texas during the morning hours, which allowed for a warm front to mix its way north across Northcentral and Northeast Texas to along the Arkansas/Louisiana border by afternoon. Large scale forcing increased during the afternoon and early evening over Northeast Texas where moderate instability had developed near the warm front and ahead of the remnants of Sergio, which resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms developing along and north of the warm front. Strong low level speed and directional shear developed southeast of the surface low and warm front over Northeast Texas, which allowed for several mini-supercell thunderstorms to develop and become severe. These storms produced several instances of damaging winds and large hail, and also an isolated tornado in Western Panola County southwest of Beckville. These storms weakened by the mid evening hours with increasing stability with the loss of heating, and the departure of forcing aloft associated with the remnants of Sergio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell near the Teaselville community.

In the wake of the flooding rains, a supercell thunderstorm developed in western Terry County along an outflow boundary leftover from the morning rain.  After producing a five-minute tornado west of Brownfield over open land, this storm crawled east for the next 1.5 hours with severe hail at times.

A positive tilted upper level system in the southwest U.S. has allowed a surface low pressure to develop across parts of southern New Mexico. As the main surface low moved east-northeast across the Texas South Plains, a band of heavier snow developed on the NW side of the low pressure where the best deformation occurred. This band of heavy snow just about split the CWA in half starting in Oldham and Hartley counties in the western Panhandles and then extending northeast into the northeast Texas Panhandle and eastern Oklahoma Panhandle. Heaviest snowfall totals of 6-9 occurred in the heaviest band of snowfall with less amounts across the rest of the region before system moved out the afternoon hours of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 6-7 of snowfall in Panhandle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of November 7th across Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and Southwest Arkansas, along a cold front and associated upper level trough. Enough shear and instability were in place to yield an isolated severe thunderstorm over Northern Red River County Texas, where strong winds downed a tree across Highway 37 near the Dimple Quick Stop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Highway 37 near the Dimple Quick Stop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of November 12th, a deep upper trough was located across the Inter-Mountain West into the Great Plains and this feature was able to tap a vigorous plume of moisture from the Eastern Pacific and the Gulf of Mexico into the Four State Region. A surface frontal boundary had pushed well south of the area and was located near or along the Southeast Texas, Southern Louisiana Gulf Coast. Just above the surface near the 5000 foot level, an elevated boundary was pushed northward during the overnight hours of the 11th and into the morning hours of the 12th. Isentropic lift became maximized along and just to the north of this elevated boundary such that portions of East Texas and Northern Louisiana near the Interstate 20 corridor saw training bands of thunderstorms, some of which produced excessive heavy rainfall which resulted in flooding. Widespread rainfall amounts of three to six inches of rain fell across these areas, atop already saturated grounds from above normal rainfall that fell since the first week of September. This resulted in numerous roads to become flooded and closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FM 855 was flooded and closed just north of the Cuney community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mesa Vista Wildfire began about nineteen miles south southwest of Wolf Creek Park in Roberts County around 1745CST just east off of Texas State Highway 70 and just south of N River Road. There were no reports of any homes or other structures that were damaged or lost from the wildfire and also there were no reports of any injuries or fatalities. The Texas A&M Forest Service responded to the wildfire which was contained by 0600CST on November 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level trough moved into the Southern Plains during the late afternoon, evening and overnight hours of November 30th-December 1st. Upper level forcing in response to this disturbance moved into the Middle Red River Valley of Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas during the evening hours of November 30th and strong to severe thunderstorms erupted north and west of the Interstate 30 Corridor as a result. The atmosphere was moderately unstable for the last day in November but strong deep layer and low level directional shear were present. This resulted in supercell type thunderstorms and bowing thunderstorm complexes, one of which moved across Northeast Texas into Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas producing widespread wind damage all along its track. Numerous trees and power lines were downed with these storms before they exited Northeast Texas during the early morning hours of December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four power poles were broken in half across Highway 37 north of the Mount Vernon ISD School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated afternoon thunderstorms produced localized microburst wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey conducted by the National Weather Service determined that a microburst which produced an estimated 75 MPH wind gust resulted in tree and structural damage near the city of Chico, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some of the Central Texas counties were limited to moderate drought (D1) until the last few days of the month, when conditions finally deteriorated to severe drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate drought conditions deteriorated to severe drought by the end of the month due to below-normal rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a very moist airmass situated across West Central Texas.  This clash in airmasses caused some flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water entered the Haskell Elementary School and one residential home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper and surface trough, an abundance of Gulf of Mexico moisture, and remnants of moisture from a dying Pacific tropical cyclone resulted in devastating flash flooding in part of West Central Texas.  The town of Sonora along the Dry Devil's Draw was hardest hit by a wall of water and subsequent flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain of eight plus inches fell north of Sonora in the vicinity of thirteen uncontrolled dams north of Sonora. Sites 1-8 are on the Dry Devils River and Sites 9-13 are on Lowrey Draw.  As these dams filled, flood waters overflowed the spillways into the draws.  This was the first time (as far as anyone knows) the emergency spillways were activated for some of these dams.  There were no breaches, these structures worked as they were meant to function.  Normally these structures would help mitigate flooding in Sonora, but previous rains had already filled up many of the sites. ||One of the deputies saw a wall of water come down the Dry Devil's Draw just north of Sonora at about 1 PM CDT near U.S. 277.  This wall of water flooded many homes located near the Dry Devil's Draw in Sonora shortly after 1 PM CDT.  Flood waters even  flowed over the US 277 bridge on the south side of Sonora.  The depth of this wall of water is estimated at around 25 feet.  The flood waters cut off the city of Sonora from neighboring first responders in all directions who wanted to help.  Emergency management reported about 250 homes were either damaged or destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two brothers drowned off of Galveston Island due to being caught up within a strong Gulf of Mexico rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 11 year-old boy got caught in a rip current and drown near 17th Street at the Seawall. His 16 year-old boy went to attempt a rescue but also drown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level disturbances interacted with plenty of gulf moisture to produce rounds of very heavy rain.  In fact, very heavy rainfall moved north into Kimble County from Edwards County, up the South Llano River watershed area during the early morning hours of Monday, October 8.  ||About eight to 12 inches of rain fell on already saturated ground, causing major flooding over the banks of Cedar Creek and the South Llano River.  A ranging flash flood on the South Llano flooded out an RV Park located on the South Llano River in Junction, drowning four campers.  The water level at the RV Park on the South Llano River was estimated at around 21 feet.  Earlier in the morning, the South Llano River at telegraph had risen to 35 feet before the gauge failed.  The gauge reading at the Llano River near Junction measured 31.53 feet on October 8.||Additional heavy rain fell across West Central Texas and resulted in flooding and flash flooding across the area.  The following counties received a Presidential Disaster Declaration for the September through October flooding:  Brown, Callahan, Edwards, Haskell, Jones, Kimble, Mason, Nolan, San Saba, Shackelord, Sutton, Taylor and Throckmorton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire department reported 12  homes under water at Lake Stamford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance interacted with a weak front and plenty of low level moisture to produce a cyclic supercell thundestorm during the early morning hours.  This cyclic supercell thunderstorm produced a tornado near Christoval and another one in Brady.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service team found evidence of tornado damage about 6 to 9 miles west southwest of Christoval.  The strong tornadic winds snapped trunks and uprooted many Live Oak and Cedar trees.  The tornado hit a metal building.  The twister removed much of the roof covering, blew out a large garage door and caused steel perlins to buckle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system remained park across Baja California through October 17.  This feature interacted with plenty of moisture and resulted in periods of very heavy rain from October 15 through the 17.  ||Widespread flooding developed across the area and many lakes and rivers rose above flood stage and resulted in widespread flooding.  Millions of dollars of damage was done to roads and county infrastructure.  ||Johnson Fork Creek rose high enough that it caused some water to overflow onto one of the lanes on Interstate 10.  Interstate 10 had to be closed.  The Clear Fork on the Brazos River near Fort Griffin rose to 36.18 feet on October 15; The Llano River at Mason rose to 32.89 foot on October 16; Colorado River near San Saba rose to 32.53 feet on October 17; Colorado River near Ballinger rose to 22.97 feet on October 17; and the San Saba River at San Saba rose to 28.54 inches on October 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: TxDOT reported RR 2596, FM 2129, and County Roads 220, 245, 300, 312, 316, 329, and 350 in Schleicher County closed due to flooding.

In the early hours of Monday morning, the normally small creek came expanded to 300 yards in size and poured volumes of water into the S. Llano.  This water seemed to have arrived just prior to the wall of water from the South Llano.  Rescue workers said they could hear the sound of a roar with a wall of water coming down the river. ||Authorities have reported that 40-45 people were evacuated and 19 people were rescued by helicopter or swift water rescue boats.  There were three men and one woman who drowned.  Some of these victims did all they could to help others evacuate the RV Park.||Cost of road damage was estimated at 9 million dollars.
